## PAGE 00425: (2026x) Removing breakpoints

- page_id: `255625693`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255625693/2026x+Removing+breakpoints
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:42:57.010+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Simulation debugging > (2026x) Breakpoints
- labels: ['removing-breakpoints']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You can remove a Breakpoint from a model using the model's **Remove Breakpoint(s)** context menu.

To remove a Breakpoint

- Right-click a model element that has a breakpoint(s) and select Simulation > Remove Breakpoint(s) .

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']

You can also click the **Remove****Breakpoint(s)** or **Remove****All****Breakpoints** toolbar button or select the **Remove Breakpoint(s)**context menu in the **Breakpoints** pane to remove all existing breakpoints. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>You can remove a Breakpoint from a model using the model's <strong>Remove Breakpoint(s)</strong> context menu. </p><p>To remove a Breakpoint</p><hr /><ul><li>Right-click a model element that has a breakpoint(s) and select <strong>Simulation</strong> &gt; <strong>Remove </strong><strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Breakpoint(s)</strong>. </li></ul><h6 style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><ac:image ac:title="Removing a Breakpoint" ac:alt="Removing a Breakpoint"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Figure_0033.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Removing breakpoints" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6><p>You can also click the <strong>Remove </strong><strong>Breakpoint(s)</strong> or <strong>Remove </strong><strong>All </strong><strong>Breakpoints</strong> toolbar button or select the <strong>Remove Breakpoint(s) </strong>context menu in the <strong>Breakpoints</strong> pane to remove all existing breakpoints. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Removing Breakpoints Using the Breakpoints Pane Context Menu" ac:alt="Removing Breakpoints Using the Breakpoints Pane Context Menu"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Figure_0034.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Removing breakpoints" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255626016 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00426: (2026x) Representing data from a CSV file in a line chart

- page_id: `255626016`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255626016/2026x+Representing+data+from+a+CSV+file+in+a+line+chart
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:43:23.128+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Simulation Configuration and UI modeling > (2026x) Time Series Chart
- labels: ['csv-file-data-in-time-series-chart', 'showing-csv-file-data']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1418925668

1418925670

1418925669

The Time series chart can read data from a CSV file and represent them in a curved line in the chart. The property External CSV in the Time series chart enables you to import an external CSV file and load data from the file.

The time Series Chart can read a CSV file with data in multiple columns and show them in lines in the chart. (The first column is always the value of time on the y-axis) and read multiple CSV files and show them all at once.

To show data from a CSV file in the Time series chart

1. Click the Load Data... button on the toolbar of the Time series chart's plot pane. [ATTACHMENT filename='loaddata_button.png']
2. Select a CSV file (see an example of the result data in a CSV file), and the Time series chart will show data from the columns in the file in lines. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] The following figure shows a line chart. The solid line in the chart represents the values plotted Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit while the dashed lines represent the values from the CSV file. [IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### A line chart with the solid line representing the plotted values and dashed lines representing the values from the CSV file.

1418925667

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Exporting plots data to a CSV file' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) CSV export' space='']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255626038 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00427: (2026x) Representing enumeration values

- page_id: `255626038`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255626038/2026x+Representing+enumeration+values
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:43:26.213+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Simulation Configuration and UI modeling > (2026x) ImageSwitcher and ActiveImage
- labels: ['representing-enumeration-values', 'enumeration-value-representation']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

###### To use the ImageSwitcher and ActiveImage to represent an enumeration value

1. Create an ImageSwitcher element, and specify the enumeration, whose values will be represented by an ActiveImage of the ImageSwitcher, as the tagged value of the represent tag definition of the ImageSwitcher element.
2. Create an Active image for each enumeration literal owned by the enumeration.
3. Specify an image that will be the image attribute of each created ActiveImage, and set the enumeration literal that will be represented by the ActiveImage as the activeElement tag definition.

You can use*SmallTestSamples.mdzip* as your example. This file is a sample project of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit. It is located in the *<*install_root*>\samples\simulation* folder. In this example, an ImageSwitcher was created to represent a VerdictKind enumeration. An ActiveImage was created for each enumeration literal of the VerdictKind as follows

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Image switcher and Active Image representing Enumeration and Enumeration Literals.

If a property is typed by an enumeration, you can use the ImageSwitcher and the ActiveImage to represent it with a UI frame mockup of the Classifier that owns the property. Once you drag the property to the panel owned by the UI frame mockup, Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will prompt you to select either an ImageSwitcher or a group of radio buttons to represent the value. If you select the ImageSwitcher, the ActiveImage that represents the enumeration literal, which is the default value of the property, will open in the panel or in the group box to which the property has been dragged.

The figure below shows the User Interface Modeling diagram of the *SmallTestSamples.mdzip* sample project. It contains a UI frame that represents the **Test Result** dialog, which is the Classifier owning the property typed by the VerdictKind enumeration. The property is **result:VerdictKind,**whose default value is **pass**. Now, the ActiveImage that represents the value **pass** will be shown in the panel.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### UI Modeling diagram containing a UI frame with Image Switcher.

When the UI mockup opens in the MagicDraw window during model simulation, the image shown in the panel containing the ImageSwitcher will be the ActiveImage that represents the enumeration literal, which is the runtime value of the property.

You can simulate the Test Result dialog in the *SmallTestSamples.mdzip* sample project. You can also change a result value by selecting one of the radio buttons that represents the value: pass, fail, inconclusive, or error. Now the image that corresponds to the value will appear.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### UI Mockup whowing the property's value typed by enumeration.

You can view more information on reusing ImageSwitcher in the UI mockup in [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Nested UI Configuration stereotype' space=''].

The image that appears on the web UI through ImageSwitcher is the same image that represents the current State of simulation UI. If the image on the simulation UI is changed, the image on the Web UI will change accordingly and vice versa. Setting the **show empty image for unspecified value** option (true/ false) on the «ImageSwitcher» will change the image on the web UI through ImageSwitcher to be the same as the image that represents the simulation UI.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The generated HTML button on the toolbar of the UI diagram.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### ImageSwitcher on the web UI representing the enumeration value that corresponds to the simulation UI.

Another more comprehensive and realistic sample is available in Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit is *Blackjack.mdzip*. This sample contains a Blackjack game simulation in which Players can only stand or hit, and the Dealer can stand on 17 or higher. Once you run the Run Blackjack Simulation Config, the Blackjack game will begin by showing a Blackjack game table UI (see the following blackjack figure).

Note that this sample also demonstrates the **Nested UI Configuration** feature (see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Nested UI Configuration stereotype' space=''] for more information). As the Dealer/Player has 7 possible card slots, according to the **Nested** **UI** **Configuration** feature, the Dealer/Player thus has 7 corresponding Value Properties, e.g., 'Card1', 'Card2', 'Card3', and so on.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### UI showing Blackjack Table in the Blackjack.mdzip sample.

The image showing the card slots demonstrates the application of ImageSwitcher and enumeration. Each card slot is actually a UI Panel having the **NestedUIConfig** stereotype applied in which the **NestedUIConfig****'s** **config** tag is set to the **Card Image Switcher** ImageSwitcher, and the **NestedUIConfig****'s** **feature** tag is set to the corresponding Value Property, e.g., 'Card1'. The **Card Image Switcher**ImageSwitcher is set to represent the **Card** **Value** enumeration. The **Card** **Image** **Switcher** ImageSwitcher contains 53 ActiveImages, each of which is the corresponding Enumeration Literal of the **Card Image** enumeration. Note the **Card Value** enumeration also contains 53 Enumeration Literals.

- '0' Enumeration Literal represents a blank image; a card slot without any card.
- '1' - '52' Enumeration Literals represent the images of 52 distinct cards.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Image Switcher containing active image of 52 cards and blank image.

When the value of one of the 7 Value Properties changes, the card slot image will change accordingly. See the Player's cards in the figure to better understand how it works as follows

- The value of the Player's 'Card1' Value Property is '49' Enumeration Literal, which is the activeElement of the '49' ActiveImage. The '49' ActiveImage represents the ten of spades.
- The value of the Player's 'Card2' Value Property is '28' Enumeration Literal, which is the activeElement of the '28' ActiveImage. The '28' ActiveImage represents the two of hearts.
- The value of the Player's 'Card3' Value Property is '0' Enumeration Literal, which represents a blank card slot. Therefore, no card is open next to the two of hearts card.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### UI and Variables panel showing the value of 7 value properties and the displayed image.

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) ImageSwitcher and ActiveImage' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Representing object states' space='']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255625719 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00428: (2026x) Representing object states

- page_id: `255625719`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255625719/2026x+Representing+object+states
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:42:59.354+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Simulation Configuration and UI modeling > (2026x) ImageSwitcher and ActiveImage
- labels: ['representing-object-states', 'state-representation']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You can use an ActiveImage to represent each state of a runtime object whose Classifier is represented by the ImageSwitcher that owns the ActiveImage. The *FlashingLight.mdzip* sample is used throughout this page. You can open the sample by downloading it from the attached link.

To use the ImageSwitcher and ActiveImage to represent a state

1. Create an ImageSwitcher element, and set its represent tag definition with a Classifier whose state will be represented by an active image owned by the ImageSwitcher.
2. Create an ActiveImage in the ImageSwitcher for each State of the Classifier represented by the ImageSwitcher.
3. Specify the image that will be the image attribute of each created ActiveImage and set the State, which will be represented by the ActiveImage, as the tagged value of the activeElement tag definition.

The following figure illustrates an example of how to use ImageSwitcher and ActiveImage to represent the States of a runtime object (see the *FlashingLight.mdzip* sample)

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Using ImageSwitcher in the FlashingLight.mdzip sample with internal simulation clock.

The image that appears on the web UI through ImageSwitcher is the same image that represents the current State of simulation UI. If the image on the simulation UI is changed, the image on the Web UI will change accordingly and vice versa. Either image is able to accept a signal by clicking the mouse.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Setting startWebServer to true in the Simulation Config to show the web UI.

###### Sample model

The model used in the figures of this page is the FlashingLight.mdzip sample model that comes with MagicDraw. To open this sample, do either of the following

- Download [ATTACHMENT filename='FlashingLight.mdzip'] .
- Find it in the modeling tool <*install_root*>\samples\simulation\FlashingLight.mdzip.

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) ImageSwitcher and ActiveImage' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Representing enumeration values' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p>You can use an ActiveImage to represent each state of a runtime object whose Classifier is represented by the ImageSwitcher that owns the ActiveImage. The <em>FlashingLight.mdzip</em> sample is used throughout this page. You can open the sample by downloading it from the attached link.</p><p><br /></p><p>To use the ImageSwitcher and ActiveImage to represent a state</p><hr /><ol><li>Create an ImageSwitcher element, and set its <strong>represent</strong> tag definition with a Classifier whose state will be represented by an active image owned by the ImageSwitcher.</li><li>Create an ActiveImage in the ImageSwitcher for each State of the Classifier represented by the ImageSwitcher.</li><li>Specify the image that will be the <strong>image</strong> attribute of each created ActiveImage and set the State, which will be represented by the ActiveImage, as the tagged value of the <strong>activeElement</strong> tag definition. </li></ol><p><br /></p><p>The following figure illustrates an example of how to use ImageSwitcher and ActiveImage to represent the States of a runtime object (see the <em>FlashingLight.mdzip</em> sample)</p><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="6742-1.PNG"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Representing object states" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Using ImageSwitcher in the FlashingLight.mdzip sample with internal simulation clock.</h6><p>The image that appears on the web UI through ImageSwitcher is the same image that represents the current State of simulation UI. If the image on the simulation UI is changed, the image on the Web UI will change accordingly and vice versa. Either image is able to accept a signal by clicking the mouse.</p><p><br /></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="6742-2.PNG"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Representing object states" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Setting startWebServer to true in the Simulation Config to show the web UI.</h6><h4>Sample model</h4><p>The model used in the figures of this page is the FlashingLight.mdzip sample model that comes with MagicDraw. To open this sample, do either of the following</p><hr /><ul><li>Download <ac:link><ri:attachment ri:filename="FlashingLight.mdzip"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Representing object states" /></ri:attachment></ac:link>.</li><li>Find it in the modeling tool <em>&lt;<em style="text-align: left;">install_root</em>&gt;\samples\simulation\FlashingLight.mdzip.</em></li></ul></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) ImageSwitcher and ActiveImage" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Representing enumeration values" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255625874 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00429: (2026x) Representing parts of a simulation context

- page_id: `255625874`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255625874/2026x+Representing+parts+of+a+simulation+context
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:43:11.635+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Simulation Configuration and UI modeling > (2026x) Nested UI Configuration stereotype
- labels: ['displaying-parts-of-simulation-context', 'showing-parts-of-simulation-context', 'simulation-context']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

A UI Panel or UI Group Box to which a NestedUIConfig stereotype is applied and a feature tag set can represent some parts and nest other components. One of the samples that shows such purpose is the *SmallTestSamples.mdzip* sample, which is located in the *<*install_root*>\samples\simulation* folder.

The following System Class diagram shows the structures of Class system and Class monitor

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Structures of Class system and Class monitor.

On the same Class diagram, there are Instance Specifications of Class system and Class monitor that will be used in the simulation as follows

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Instance Specifications of Class system and Class monitor.

The User Interface Modeling diagram displays the UI configuration that will be used in the simulation as follows

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### UI Configuration of Class system.

The following figure displays the UI configuration and the Specification of the UI Panel named panel1, which represents the monitor1 Property, as a part of Class system

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
UI Configuration and its Specification.

When the **test_nested_panels** SimulationConfig is run, the UI mockup will be displayed. The following figure exhibits the UI Panels and UI Group Boxes that represent the parts (Properties) of the Class system and in-depth nested parts as well 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The Simulation Variables panel and Runtime UI Mockup.

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255625860 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00430: (2026x) Representing parts using existing UI Configurations

- page_id: `255625860`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255625860/2026x+Representing+parts+using+existing+UI+Configurations
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:43:10.851+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Simulation Configuration and UI modeling > (2026x) Nested UI Configuration stereotype
- labels: ['displaying-parts-using-ui-configurations', 'ui-configurations']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You can also use a NestedUIConfig stereotype to show a part using existing UI Configurations. Any UI Panels or UI Group Boxes that will be used for this purpose must be applied with the NestedUIConfig stereotype and must also be set for both the feature and Config tags. The benefit of this purpose is that you can reuse existing UI Configurations to illustrate any parts of contexts that have the same Class represented. Thus, you do not need to create a new UI Configuration whenever you want to represent another part that has the same Class represented.

The FlashLight sample will be used to show how to model a part using an existing UI Configuration. The following steps show you how to create a UI Mockup that represents the entire system parts, which uses only one UI Frame

1. Open the FlashLight sample, which is located in the *<*install_root*>/samples/simulation* directory (*FlashingLight.mdzip*). The following System Definition Class diagram shows the definition of the FlashLight system. By default, when executing and running this sample, you will see the Button and Light are shown in different Frames. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] The following figure demonstrates the runtime User Interface Mockup that represents the Class Button and Class Light [IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. Right-click the Containment tree and select New Diagram > Other Diagrams > User Interface Modeling Diagram to create a User Interface Modeling diagram.
3. Create a UI Frame on the UI Modeling diagram by dragging the Class system to the newly created UI Frame as follows [IMAGE alt='' src='']
4. Create two UI panels in the UI Frame.
5. Drag the Light property to one of the UI panels to apply the NestedUIConfig stereotype to the UI Panel and set the **Light** property to the feature tag of that UI panel as follows [IMAGE alt='' src='']
6. Drag an existing UI Configuration named LampBulb to the UI panel that represents the Light property. This will set the dragged UI Configuration to the Config tag of that UI panel as follows [IMAGE alt='' src=''] After assigning the LampBulb UI Configuration to the UI panel representing the Light Property, you will see the tagged value Specification of the UI panel that represents the Light property in the Specification of the UI Panel dialog as follows [IMAGE alt='' src='']
7. Drag the Button property to another UI panel. Now the UI panel represents the Button property as part of Class system. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
8. Drag an existing UI Configuration named PowerButton to the UI panel that represents the Button property. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
9. Create a new Simulation Configuration element on any Simulation Configuration diagrams; set the simulationTarget tag to Class system and set the UI tag to the UI Frame that represents the Class system. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
10. Simulate and run the newly created Simulation Configuration. The UI Mockup will appear as illustrated in the following figure

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>You can also use a NestedUIConfig stereotype to show a part using existing UI Configurations. Any UI Panels or UI Group Boxes that will be used for this purpose must be applied with the NestedUIConfig stereotype and must also be set for both the feature and Config tags. The benefit of this purpose is that you can reuse existing UI Configurations to illustrate any parts of contexts that have the same Class represented. Thus, you do not need to create a new UI Configuration whenever you want to represent another part that has the same Class represented. </p><p>The FlashLight sample will be used to show how to model a part using an existing UI Configuration. The following steps show you how to create a UI Mockup that represents the entire system parts, which uses only one UI Frame <span style="color: rgb(125,133,154);"> <br /></span></p><ol><li><p>Open the FlashLight sample, which is located in the <em>&lt;<em style="text-align: left;">install_root</em>&gt;/samples/simulation</em> directory (<em>FlashingLight.mdzip</em>). The following System Definition Class diagram shows the definition of the FlashLight system. By default, when executing and running this sample, you will see the Button and Light are shown in different Frames. </p><h6><ac:image ac:title="System Definition Class Diagram of the FlashLight Sample" ac:alt="System Definition Class Diagram of the FlashLight Sample"><ri:attachment ri:filename="NestedUI_6.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Representing parts using existing UI Configurations" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6><p>The following figure demonstrates the runtime User Interface Mockup that represents the Class Button and Class Light</p><h6><ac:image ac:title="The Runtime UI Mockup of Class Button and Light" ac:alt="The Runtime UI Mockup of Class Button and Light"><ri:attachment ri:filename="NestedUI_7.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Representing parts using existing UI Configurations" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6></li><li>Right-click the Containment tree and select <strong>New</strong> <strong>Diagram</strong> &gt; <strong>Other</strong> <strong>Diagrams</strong> &gt; <strong>User</strong> <strong>Interface </strong><strong>Modeling</strong> <strong>Diagram</strong> to create a User Interface Modeling diagram.</li><li><p>Create a UI Frame on the UI Modeling diagram by dragging the Class system to the newly created UI Frame as follows</p><h6><ac:image ac:title="The UI Frame Representing Class System" ac:alt="The UI Frame Representing Class System"><ri:attachment ri:filename="UIFrameRepresentingClassSystem.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Representing parts using existing UI Configurations" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6></li><li>Create two UI panels in the UI Frame.</li><li><p>Drag the Light property to one of the UI panels to apply the NestedUIConfig stereotype to the UI Panel and set the <strong>Light</strong> property to the feature tag of that UI panel as follows</p><h6><ac:image ac:title="The UI Panel Representing the Light Property as a Part of Class System" ac:alt="The UI Panel Representing the Light Property as a Part of Class System"><ri:attachment ri:filename="NestedUI_9.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Representing parts using existing UI Configurations" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6></li><li><p>Drag an existing UI Configuration named LampBulb to the UI panel that represents the Light property. This will set the dragged UI Configuration to the Config tag of that UI panel as follows</p><h6><ac:image ac:title="Assigning the LampBulb UI Configuration to the UI Panel Representing the Light Property" ac:alt="Assigning the LampBulb UI Configuration to the UI Panel Representing the Light Property"><ri:attachment ri:filename="NestedUI_10.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Representing parts using existing UI Configurations" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6><p>After assigning the LampBulb UI Configuration to the UI panel representing the Light Property, you will see the tagged value Specification of the UI panel that represents the Light property in the Specification of the UI Panel dialog as follows</p><h6><ac:image ac:title="Tagged Value Specification of the UI Panel Representing the Light Property" ac:alt="Tagged Value Specification of the UI Panel Representing the Light Property"><ri:attachment ri:filename="spec-of-panel.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Representing parts using existing UI Configurations" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6><p><br /></p></li><li><p>Drag the Button property to another UI panel. Now t<span style="color: rgb(112,112,112);">he UI panel represents the Button property as part of Class system.</span></p><h6><ac:image ac:title="The UI Panel Representing the Button PropertyAs Part of Class System" ac:alt="The UI Panel Representing the Button PropertyAs Part of Class System"><ri:attachment ri:filename="NestedUI_12.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Representing parts using existing UI Configurations" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6></li><li><p>Drag an existing UI Configuration named PowerButton to the UI panel that represents the Button property.</p><h6><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="NestedUI_13.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Representing parts using existing UI Configurations" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6></li><li><p>Create a new Simulation Configuration element on any Simulation Configuration diagrams; set the simulationTarget tag to Class system and set the UI tag to the UI Frame that represents the Class system.</p><h6><ac:image ac:title="Execution Configuration Elements and Its Specificatons" ac:alt="Execution Configuration Elements and Its Specificatons"><ri:attachment ri:filename="flashlight-config.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Representing parts using existing UI Configurations" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6></li><li>Simulate and run the newly created Simulation Configuration. The UI Mockup will appear as illustrated in the following figure</li></ol><h6 style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="The UI Mockup Representing Class System and Its Parts" ac:alt="The UI Mockup Representing Class System and Its Parts"><ri:attachment ri:filename="NestedUI_15.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Representing parts using existing UI Configurations" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6><p><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255624824 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00431: (2026x) Requirement and test case traceability

- page_id: `255624824`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255624824/2026x+Requirement+and+test+case+traceability
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:42:06.161+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Simulation of SysML models > (2026x) Requirements traceability from the Variables pane
- labels: ['test-case-traceability']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

During a model simulation, Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit lists all constraints and requirements or test cases that are related in the model in the Console pane. When the simulation fails to satisfy or fulfill some constraints, it will cause the model simulation to fail and errors to occur. You can see the error messages and the links in the **Console** pane and **Variables** pane, to navigate to the requirement(s) that cause the test case to fail.

A tooltip message of a failed test case in the **Variables** pane is in the example as follows.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Tracing to Failed Constraints.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>During a model simulation, Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit lists all constraints and requirements or test cases that are related in the model in the Console pane. When the simulation fails to satisfy or fulfill some constraints, it will cause the model simulation to fail and errors to occur. You can see the error messages and the links in the <strong>Console</strong> pane and <strong>Variables</strong> pane, to navigate to the requirement(s) that cause the test case to fail. </p><p>A tooltip message of a failed test case in the <strong>Variables</strong> pane is in the example as follows. </p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Tracing to Failed Constraints" ac:alt="Tracing to Failed Constraints"><ri:attachment ri:filename="link_toReq.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Requirement and test case traceability" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br />Tracing to Failed Constraints.</h6>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255624781 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00432: (2026x) Requirement refined by a constraint block

- page_id: `255624781`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255624781/2026x+Requirement+refined+by+a+constraint+block
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:42:03.233+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Simulation of SysML models > (2026x) Requirements traceability from the Variables pane
- labels: ['requirement-refined-by-constraint-block']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

If you use a constraint block, which refines a SysML requirement, to type a constraint property of a block, you can select the object of the constraint property to navigate into a SysML requirement that is related to that object in the Containment tree. 
 
To navigate to a SysML requirement related to a constraint property in the Containment tree

1. Right click a constraint property object in the Variables pane and select Go To (see the following figure).
2. Select the name of a requirement that appears on the sub menu. Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will show you the SysML requirement that is related to the constraint property specified by the selected object in the Containment tree. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] Navigating into a SysML Requirement related to a Constraint Property.

The preceding figure shows the block **Circle** whose constraint property typed by the constraint block **Maximum****Area**, which refines the requirement **Maximum****Area**. It also shows you that if you right-click the object that specifies the constraint property when Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit is executing the block **Circle**, and select the **Go****To** context menu, you will see the requirement **Maximum****Area**. Selecting the requirement on the menu will highlight that requirement in the Containment tree.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>If you use a constraint block, which refines a SysML requirement, to type a constraint property of a block, you can select the object of the constraint property to navigate into a SysML requirement that is related to that object in the Containment tree. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" />To navigate to a SysML requirement related to a constraint property in the Containment tree </p><hr /><ol><li>Right click a constraint property object in the <strong>Variables</strong> pane and select <strong>Go</strong> <strong>To</strong> (see the following figure).</li><li><p>Select the name of a requirement that appears on the sub menu. Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will show you the SysML requirement that is related to the constraint property specified by the selected object in the Containment tree. </p><h6><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Navigating into a SysML Requirement related to a Constraint Property" ac:alt="Navigating into a SysML Requirement related to a Constraint Property"><ri:attachment ri:filename="navigate_sysmlRequirement.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Requirement refined by a constraint block" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6><h6 style="text-align: center;">Navigating into a SysML Requirement related to a Constraint Property.</h6></li></ol><p>The preceding figure shows the block <strong>Circle</strong> whose constraint property typed by the constraint block <strong>Maximum </strong><strong>Area</strong>, which refines the requirement <strong>Maximum </strong><strong>Area</strong>. It also shows you that if you right-click the object that specifies the constraint property when Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit is executing the block <strong>Circle</strong>, and select the <strong>Go </strong><strong>To</strong> context menu, you will see the requirement <strong>Maximum </strong><strong>Area</strong>. Selecting the requirement on the menu will highlight that requirement in the Containment tree. </p><p> </p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255624777 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00433: (2026x) Requirement satisfied by a property

- page_id: `255624777`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255624777/2026x+Requirement+satisfied+by+a+property
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:42:02.867+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Simulation of SysML models > (2026x) Requirements traceability from the Variables pane
- labels: ['requirement-satisfied-by-property']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You can also select a value that specifies a property in the **Variables** pane to navigate into a SysML Requirement, which is satisfied by the property, in the Containment tree. When you right-click the value and select the **Go** **To** context menu, you will see a specific Requirement satisfied by the property.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Navigating into a Requirement satisfied by a Property.

Simulation Toolkit supports contextualized satisfy relationship that value property from inner nested structure is connected to Requirements. For example, the *constraint on value* Requirement must be satisfied by the *length of the width of Rectangle* object only in this context (not the *length of the height of Rectangle*, *length of Square*, or *length of Line* objects) through the **Source Context** and **Source Property Path** tags as shown in the figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Contextualized satisfy relationship through the Source Context and Source Property Path tags.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>You can also select a value that specifies a property in the <strong>Variables</strong> pane to navigate into a SysML Requirement, which is satisfied by the property, in the Containment tree. When you right-click the value and select the <strong>Go</strong> <strong>To</strong> context menu, you will see a specific Requirement satisfied by the property. </p><p><br /></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Navigating into a Requirement Satisfied by a Property" ac:alt="Navigating into a Requirement Satisfied by a Property"><ri:attachment ri:filename="navigate_reqtypedbyProperty.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Requirement satisfied by a property" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br />Navigating into a Requirement satisfied by a Property.</h6><p>Simulation Toolkit supports contextualized satisfy relationship that value property from inner nested structure is connected to Requirements. For example, the <em>constraint on value</em> Requirement must be satisfied by the <em>length of the width of Rectangle</em> object only in this context (not the <em>length of the height of Rectangle</em>, <em>length of Square</em>, or <em>length of Line</em> objects) through the <strong>Source Context</strong> and <strong>Source Property Path</strong> tags as shown in the figure below.</p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Contextualized Satisfy Relationship.PNG"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Requirement satisfied by a property" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Contextualized satisfy relationship through the Source Context and Source Property Path tags.</h6>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255624775 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00434: (2026x) Requirements traceability from the Variables pane

- page_id: `255624775`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255624775/2026x+Requirements+traceability+from+the+Variables+pane
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:42:02.513+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Simulation of SysML models
- labels: ['requirement-traceability']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

When you model with SysML, you can create a relationship between a SysML requirement and any element in your model. Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit allows you to navigate from the runtime value or from the object, to the related SysML requirement during the model simulation.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>When you model with SysML, you can create a relationship between a SysML requirement and any element in your model. Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit allows you to navigate from the runtime value or from the object, to the related SysML requirement during the model simulation. </p><p><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255624556 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00435: (2026x) Results Player

- page_id: `255624556`
- space_key: `MMA`

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**On this page**

3

The Results Player displays numerical values from a CSV file directly in composite structure diagrams, such as Internal Block Diagram, Parametric, etc. This allows you to review the simulation results, specific time steps, and display results of an external simulation (for example, a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) SSP Export - Technology Preview' space=''].

You can view the active States for the selected parts in the Results Player when you load a CSV file. To learn more about how to export active States for the parts, refer the[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) CSV export' space=''] page.

You can view an image of the active state next to the state label in the Results Player. Each state machine supports only one active state; Composite and orthogonal states are not supported. To learn more, refer to the[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Displaying simulation information' space=''] page.

#### NOTE: Prerequisites for displaying Active State Images

Prerequisites for displaying Active State Images

- The StateMachine must be set as Classifier Behavior.
- Only a single state must be active at a time.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The Results Player when a file is loaded.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Active State Image displayed when the file is loaded in the Results Player.

##### Understanding the UI components of the Results Player

The following table describes the UI components of the Results Player:

| Icon | Name | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
|  | Load/Unload File | Selects a CSV export file to load/Deselects the CSV export file. |
|  | Beginning | Stops the play and jumps to the first step (Shortcut key: Home). |
|  | Step Backward | Jumps to the previous step. When the CTRL key is held on the Keyboard, jumps to the previous failed step. |
|  | Play | Starts automatic playback of time values in real-time (Shortcut key: Space). |
|  | Pause | Pauses the playback of time values in real-time (Shortcut key: Space). |
|  | Step Forward | Jumps to the next step. When the CTRL key is held on the Keyboard, jumps to the next failed step. |
|  | End | Stops the play and jumps to the last step (Shortcut key: End). |
|  | Playback speed | Controls the playback speed. |
|  | Slider | Displays the current position and allows you to manually scroll through the values. |
|  | Time indicator | Shows the current step and allows you to manually enter the desired time value. |
|  | Time Series Chart | Generates the Time Series Chart. |
|  | Export to Instance | Generates the Instance. |
|  | Verify Requirements | Verifies the requirements present in the loaded CSV file.The green dot indicates that verification is successful and no requirement has failed.The red dot indicates that some of the requirements have failed. |
|  | The green dot indicates that verification is successful and no requirement has failed. |  |
|  | The red dot indicates that some of the requirements have failed. |  |

##### Launching the Results Player

You can launch the Results Player both through the Containment tree and the composite structure diagram.

To launch the Results Player

- In the Containment tree, right-click the composite structure diagram and select Tools> Results Player . [ATTACHMENT filename='Result Player_Containement Tree.png']
- In the composite structure diagram, right-click anywhere and select Tools>Results Player. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Loading CSV export file

#### NOTE: Prerequisites

Prerequisites

- The CSV file must be comma-delimited.
- The first column must contain time values. Time units can be specified in parentheses; otherwise, seconds are used. Supported time units are ns, µs, ms, s, min, h, d, mo, y.
- Other column headers must have property paths specified in dot notation, such as engine.valveTrain.speed. U nrecognized columns are ignored.

You must load the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) CSV export' space=''] file to be able to use the Results Player. You can load the CSV export file either manually or automatically after the simulation terminates.

To load the CSV export file manually

1. Click on the Load File command in the Results Player. [ATTACHMENT filename='Manually loading file.png']
2. In the Open dialog, select the CSV file and click Open . [ATTACHMENT filename='Open Dialog.png']

- You can drag and drop the CSV file from the file system on the Result Player. You can also drag and drop the CSV file from the Containment tree on the Result Player and the composite diagram.
- SysML properties are mapped to CSV file headers depending on the diagram where the file is loaded. For example, if the file has information on a System, then it must be loaded in a diagram displaying the System structure. It cannot be loaded in other level diagrams (such as Controller or Water Tank). Results can also be seen in the nested structure diagrams.

To load a CSV export file automatically when a simulation terminates:

1. In the Specification of CSV export dialog, set the Load in Player property to true. [IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Generating a Time Series Chart

You can generate a Time Series Chart with help of Results Player.

To generate a Time Series Chart

1. Click the [IMAGE alt='' src=''] icon, found on the Result Player.
2. From the contextual menu, select the required values and click**OK**. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src=''] While selecting values in the dialog, you can add up to ten values at a time. If there are more than ten values, click **Select All**to****select the initial ten values in the dialog.

The generated Time Series Chart is displayed for the selected values. You can also change/update the values by clicking the [IMAGE alt='' src=''] icon in the Time Series Chart. You can generate multiple charts with different values at the same time. When a CSV file is unloaded, charts are deactivated, but remain open for a reference. Additionally, you can load a new CSV export file and generate new Time Series Charts. A black vertical line indicates the current time step and you can change the time step by clicking on the chart. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Time Series Chart

When you export the CSV file automatically and specify the Time Series chart in the simulation configuration, the Time Series chart is generated automatically with the same values as the original configuration.

##### Exporting the current step to an Instance Specification

To export the current step to an Instance Specification

1. Click the [IMAGE alt='' src=''] icon present on the Result Player.
2. From the Selecting Owner or Instance dialog, select a Package to generate a new instance, or select an existing instance to update its values. Click**OK**. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

- The created instance is highlighted in blue in the Containment tree.
- If an instance has several classifiers set, then you will only work with one that matches your target.

##### Verifying the Requirements

You can verify requirements in the Results Player when you export the CSV file.You can verify requirements that are specified with the help of the Requirement patterns; however, the requirements specified using a constraint block are not supported. To learn more about how to use the requirement patterns, refer to the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Using Requirement patterns glossary' space='MT'] glossary.

To verify the requirements

1. Load the CSV export file. To learn more, refer to section.
2. Click [ATTACHMENT filename='Verify Requirements_Symbol.png'] icon present on the Results Player. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

The Verify Requirements command is disabled ifno requirements in the context are compatible

Once you click the Verify Requirements icon, the icon is updated with a red or green dot in it. The [IMAGE alt='' src=''] icon indicates that the verification is successful and no requirements have failed. The [IMAGE alt='' src=''] icon indicates that some of the requirements have failed.In the Block diagram, the label of a verified value is displayed green if it is passing at the current time step, and it is displayed red if it is failing at the current time step. A tooltip along with the red label is displayed for the failing value. Also, the failing regions are highlighted in red on the Slider for the failedrequirements.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### A red label with tooltip, a green label and failing region on the Slider displayed in the Block diagram and on the Results Player respectively.

Requirement verification also has a drop down menu with extra commands to increase the usability.

| Sr. No. | Icon | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 1 |  | The default action is same as the main command. |
| 2 |  | The command exports all mapped data to a new CSV file and adds a column with requirements verification results. For example, if the verified requirement is WaterLevel, then the requirements verification results column is added after the WaterLevel column. The name of the added column includes ID and name of the requirement. |
| 3 |  | Clears the verified results. |

style="vertical-align: middle;"><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">The default action is same as the main command</span>.</span></p></td></tr><tr class=""><td style="text-align: center;vertical-align: middle;"><div class="content-wrapper"><p>2</p></div></td><td style="vertical-align: middle;"><div class="content-wrapper"><p><ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="22"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Export Results to CSV.png" /></ac:image></p></div></td><td style="vertical-align: middle;"><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">The command exports all mapped data to a new CSV file and adds a column with requirements verification results. </span><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">For example, if the verified requirement is WaterLevel, then the r<span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">equirements verification results column is added </span>after the  WaterLevel column. The name of the added column includes <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">ID and </span>name <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">of the requirement.</span></span></p></td></tr><tr class=""><td style="text-align: center;vertical-align: middle;"><div class="content-wrapper"><p>3</p></div></td><td style="vertical-align: middle;"><div class="content-wrapper"><p><ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="22"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Clear Verification.png" /></ac:image></p></div></td><td style="vertical-align: middle;">Clears the verified results.</td></tr></tbody></table></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255626001 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00436: (2026x) Reusable UI Mockup

- page_id: `255626001`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255626001/2026x+Reusable+UI+Mockup
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:43:20.714+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Simulation Configuration and UI modeling
- labels: ['reusing-ui-mockup', 'ui-mockup']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1424462686

1424462688

1424462687

Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit improves the flexibility in user interface modeling. You can reuse UI components by typing the property using the UI component. The object, which is the value of the property, represents a mock-up of the UI component. Once the object is created, the mock-up UI will be created and shown in the MagicDraw window. Whenever the object is deleted, the mock-up UI will be deleted as well. It is not necessary to add the UI component to the UI tag definition of the Simulation Config stereotype.

To create and reuse a UI component

1. Create a UI Frame on a UI Modeling element diagram.
2. Drag the created UI Frame from the Containment browser to the UI Frame symbol in the UI Modeling diagram to create a UI prototype as follows [IMAGE alt='' src='']
3. Create a property in the block. Whenever this property is initialized, the UI Frame simulation will run.
4. Use the UI Frame as a type for the created property. [IMAGE alt='' src='']

You can use a UI Frame to create other properties and define it as the type of the properties. A UI Mockup will start and will be deleted with an object that is the value of the property typed by a UI component (including but not limited to the property in Step 3. You can show or hide a UI Mockup by using the context menu of the instance of the property.

To hide a UI mockup

1. Right-click an instance of a property and click Hide Mock-up in the Variable panel.
2. Click the Close button.

To show a hidden UI mockup

- Right-click an instance of a property and click Show Mock-up .

You can also show or hide the other UI components, for example, time series chart and ImageSwitcher.

1424462685

**Related page**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Creating User Interface mockups for the stopwatch model' space='']

You can reuse UI components by typing the property using the UI component. The object, which is the value of the property, represents a mock-up of the UI component. Once the object is created, the mock-up UI will be created and shown in the MagicDraw window. Whenever the object is deleted, the mock-up UI will be deleted as well. It is not necessary to add the UI component to the UI tag definition of the Simulation Config stereotype. </p><p>To create and reuse a UI component</p><hr /><ol><li>Create a UI Frame on a UI Modeling element diagram.</li><li><p>Drag the created UI Frame from the Containment browser to the UI Frame symbol in the UI Modeling diagram to create a UI prototype as follows</p><h6><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="dragUIframe.jpg"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Reusable UI Mockup" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6></li><li>Create a property in the block. Whenever this property is initialized, the UI Frame simulation will run.</li><li><p>Use the UI Frame as a type for the created property.</p><h6><ac:image ac:title="Using the UI Frame as the Property's Type" ac:alt="Using the UI Frame as the Property's Type"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Using UI frame as a property type.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Reusable UI Mockup" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6></li></ol><p>You can use a UI Frame to create other properties and define it as the type of the properties. A UI Mockup will start and will be deleted with an object that is the value of the property typed by a UI component (including but not limited to the property in Step 3. You can show or hide a UI Mockup by using the context menu of the instance of the property. </p><p>To hide a UI mockup</p><hr /><ol><li>Right-click an instance of a property and click <strong>Hide</strong> <strong>Mock-up</strong> in the <strong>Variable</strong> panel.</li><li>Click the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Close</strong> button.</li></ol><p>To show a hidden UI mockup</p><hr /><ul><li>Right-click an instance of a property and click <strong>Show</strong> <strong>Mock-up</strong>.</li></ul><p> <br class="atl-forced-newline" />You can also show or hide the other UI components, for example, time series chart and ImageSwitcher. </p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="9b712532-36d6-44f7-945b-4617b6146e75"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1424462685</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related page</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating User Interface mockups for the stopwatch model" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255625137 space=MMA version=2 -->
## PAGE 00437: (2026x) Rollup Pattern simulation

- page_id: `255625137`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255625137/2026x+Rollup+Pattern+simulation
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:47:30.689+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) Tutorial > (2026x) Analysis pattern
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

643911130

643911151

643911129

**On this page**

33

643911141

Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit supports rollup calculations of total mass, cost, power, and another system dimension, based on individual values of all the parts in the model. Please refer to [Rollup Pattern Wizard](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/Rollup+Pattern+Wizard), [applying Rollup Pattern Blocks](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/Applying+Rollup+Pattern+Blocks), and **LaptopCostAnalysis** and **SpacecraftMassRollup** built-in samples for more details.

##### Simulation with a built-in rollup pattern

The **LaptopCostAnalysis** sample applies built-in **CostRollUpPattern**. The sample uses the **Cost Analysis** Block with the **Laptop** context Block as the Part. Optionally, you can add any constraint (*maxCost*) and Requirement (*Total cost*) and use a Parametric diagram to connect them as shown in the figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The Cost Analysis Block containing the Laptop context Block applied with built-in CostRollUpPattern as the Part.

You can also either run the simulation through the **Cost Analysis** Block directly or use a [SimulationConfig](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MMA/(2026x) SimulationConfig+stereotype) with *executionTarget* *= the Cost Analysis Block*. The result will be shown in the **totalCost** context Block, e.g., 220 (80+50+90) as shown in the figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The totalCost context Block as the result of Laptop cost analysis applied with CostRollUpPattern.

##### Simulation with a custom rollup pattern

The **SpacecraftMassRollup** sample applies a custom rollup pattern through the same [applying Rollup Pattern Block](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/Applying+Rollup+Pattern+Blocks) like the built-in one but requires selecting a Pattern Block for the custom rollup pattern, e.g., **Component**. The **Component****Pattern Block**uses value type properties binding with 5 constraint properties in the Parametric diagram as shown below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### A Parametric diagram of the Component custom rollup pattern as a Pattern Block.

Optionally, you can add any constraint (*less*) and Requirement (*Estimated mass*)*,* and then run the *spacecraft mass analysis* [SimulationConfig](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MMA/(2026x) SimulationConfig+stereotype) with the *executionTarget* as an instance of the spacecraft to run. The result of evaluation, according to the *me < ma* rule, will be highlighted at *me : mass[kilogram]* for each component shown on the right side in the Block Definition diagram as shown below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The evaluation result (me : mass[kilogram]) of the SpacecraftMassRollup sample applied with the Component custom rollup pattern.

The sample uses the <strong>Cost Analysis</strong> Block with the <strong>Laptop</strong> context Block as the Part. Optionally, you can add any constraint (<em>maxCost</em>) and Requirement (<em>Total cost</em>) and use a Parametric diagram to connect them as shown in the figure below.</p><p align="center"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:width="900"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Cost Analysis Block.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Rollup Pattern simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">The Cost Analysis Block containing the Laptop context Block applied with built-in CostRollUpPattern as the Part.</h6><p>You can also either run the simulation through the <strong>Cost Analysis</strong> Block directly or use a <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MMA/(2026x) SimulationConfig+stereotype">SimulationConfig</a> with <em>executionTarget</em> <em>= the Cost Analysis Block</em>. The <strong>Component</strong><em> </em>Pattern Block<em> </em>uses value type properties binding with 5 constraint properties in the Parametric diagram as shown below.</p><p align="center"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:width="900"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Parametric Diagram of the Component.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Rollup Pattern simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">A Parametric diagram of the Component custom rollup pattern as a Pattern Block.</h6><p>Optionally, you can add any constraint (<em>less</em>) and Requirement (<em>Estimated mass</em>)<em>,</em> and then run the <em>spacecraft mass analysis</em> <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MMA/(2026x) SimulationConfig+stereotype">SimulationConfig</a> with the <em>executionTarget</em> as an instance of the spacecraft to run. The result of evaluation, according to the <em>me &lt; ma</em> rule, will be highlighted at <em>me : mass[kilogram]</em> for each component shown on the right side in the Block Definition diagram as shown below.</p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:width="900"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Evaluation Result of the SpacecraftMassRollup Sample.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Rollup Pattern simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">The evaluation result (me : mass[kilogram]) of the SpacecraftMassRollup sample applied with the Component custom rollup pattern.</h6></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255624853 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00438: (2026x) Running a Call Action simulation without a target pin

- page_id: `255624853`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255624853/2026x+Running+a+Call+Action+simulation+without+a+target+pin
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:42:08.393+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Activity simulation
- labels: ['call-action-simulation']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

375306651

375306653

375306652

When Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit simulates a CallOperationAction that does not have a target pin, it will select a runtime object, which is the context of the Activity, as a target. For example, a System Class, which owns a Sub-System Class (see the second figure below), is the context of the SystemBehavior Activity shown in the first figure. The CallOperationAction will call the SayHello operation using the Activity context, which in this case is the System Class.

If an Action belongs to an Activity partition, and the Activity partition represents part of a Classifier, which is a context of that Activity, Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will select the runtime object specified by the part as the target of the Action.

However, Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will direct the target object to the callOperationAction only if an element represented by the Activity partition is a property (or an inherited property) of the Classifier, which owns the Activity and if there is only one runtime object specified by the property represented by the Activity partition. For example, if the CallOperationAction that calls the Print operation is in the Activity partition, which represents the SubSystem property of the System Class, this will result in a call to invoke the Print operation in the SubSystem property.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Activity System Behavior. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Class diagram describing system Class structure.

375306650

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Call Operation Action' space='MT']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Activity Partition execution and allocated Behavior' space='']

For example, a System Class, which owns a Sub-System Class (see the second figure below), is the context of the SystemBehavior Activity shown in the first figure. The CallOperationAction will call the SayHello operation using the Activity context, which in this case is the System Class. </p><p>If an Action belongs to an Activity partition, and the Activity partition represents part of a Classifier, which is a context of that Activity, Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will select the runtime object specified by the part as the target of the Action. </p><p>However, Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will direct the target object to the callOperationAction only if an element represented by the Activity partition is a property (or an inherited property) of the Classifier, which owns the Activity and if there is only one runtime object specified by the property represented by the Activity partition. For example, if the CallOperationAction that calls the Print operation is in the Activity partition, which represents the SubSystem property of the System Class, this will result in a call to invoke the Print operation in the SubSystem property. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Activity System Behavior" ac:alt="Activity System Behavior"><ri:attachment ri:filename="ActivitySysBehavior.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Running a Call Action simulation without a target pin" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br />Activity System Behavior.<br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Class Diagram Describing System Class Structure" ac:alt="Class Diagram Describing System Class Structure"><ri:attachment ri:filename="ClassDescribes Sysstructure.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Running a Call Action simulation without a target pin" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br />Class diagram describing system Class structure.</h6><p> </p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="1c82bf58-e46c-4845-9379-f42f4b4e8a7b"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">375306650</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MT" ri:content-title="Call Operation Action" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Activity Partition execution and allocated Behavior" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255625669 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00439: (2026x) Running a Simulation Configuration

- page_id: `255625669`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255625669/2026x+Running+a+Simulation+Configuration
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:42:55.290+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Model simulation
- labels: ['simulating-simulation-configuration']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1333564395

1333564398

1333564396

Cameo Simulation Toolkit provides a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Simulation Configuration and UI modeling' space=''] that you can use to run a model with specified simulation properties, e.g. include Charts, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Reusable UI Mockup' space=''], specify time properties, and more.

To run a Simulation Configuration, do one of the following

- In the main toolbar, click [ATTACHMENT filename='run_button.png'] next to the Simulation Configuration name. [ATTACHMENT filename='running_simulation_configuration.png']
- In the diagram toolbar, click a small arrow next to [ATTACHMENT filename='run_button.png'] and select the Simulation Configuration you want to run . If you want to execute a specific element displayed in a diagram, select the element shape before selecting the Simulation Configuration. [ATTACHMENT filename='simconfig_from_diagram_toolbar.png']
- Right-click an element in the Containment tree or right-click the element shape in a diagram and select Simulation , then select the Simulation Configuration you want to run. [ATTACHMENT filename='simconfig_from_shortcut_menu.png']
- In the Simulation Configuration Diagram , right-click a Simulation Configuration shape and select Simulation > Run .

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']

To terminate a Simulation Configuration, do one of the following

- In the main toolbar, click the Terminate button next to the Simulation Configuration name. [ATTACHMENT filename='terminating_simulationConfig_from_main_toolbar.png']
- Click the Terminate button in the Simulation pane. [ATTACHMENT filename='terminating_simulationConfig_from_simulation_pane.png']

1333564393

**Related page**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Simulation Configuration and UI modeling' space='']

/></ri:attachment></ac:image> and select the Simulation Configuration you want to <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="2d39cd86-2663-4724-880e-67ddcbe1f7d8">run</ac:inline-comment-marker>. /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Right-click an element in the Containment tree or right-click the element shape in a diagram and select <strong>Simulation</strong>, then select the 0.0px;">Run</strong><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">. </span></li></ul><h6 style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:alt="Running a Simulation Configuration Element through the Context Menu" <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255626214 space=MMA version=2 -->
## PAGE 00440: (2026x) Running a State Machine simulation

- page_id: `255626214`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255626214/2026x+Running+a+State+Machine+simulation
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:47:29.554+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) State Machine simulation
- labels: ['executing-state-machines']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

123795415

123795417

123795416

Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will perform a State Machine simulation if the following elements are selected for the simulation

- A State Machine.
- A State Machine diagram.
- A class whose Classifier Behavior is defined by a State Machine.
- An InstanceSpecification whose Classifier is a Class that has a defined Classifier Behavior with a State Machine.

The function of Triggers is to change the State of a runtime object during a State Machine simulation. The Trigger can be either a signal or a time Event. If it is a signal Event Trigger, a signal will be sent to a runtime object to trigger it from one State to another. To send the Trigger signal, you have to select a runtime object, which is a target for the signal, in the **Variables** pane. All signals that can be received by the selected runtime object will be listed on the **Trigger** drop-down menu on the **Simulation** window toolbar as follows

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
The Trigger drop-down menu.

You can use a User Interface Mockup to send a signal to a runtime object. See more information about UI mockup in [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) UI modeling diagram simulation' space=''].

You can trigger a transition directly on a diagram. The menu **Fire Transition** on the smart manipulation toolbar triggers a transition and sends it to the activeObject that is displayed on the diagram. The **Fire Transition** command ignores guards.

To trigger a transition directly on a diagram, do either of the following

- Either select a target object from the Variables pane and right-click a transition line on the diagram, and select Fire Transition .
- During the simulation, click a transition line on the diagram and select Fire Transition .

123795413

**Related pages**

- State Machine
- State Machine diagram
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) UI modeling diagram simulation' space='']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255625085 space=MMA version=2 -->
## PAGE 00441: (2026x) Running server-side simulation with SSL

- page_id: `255625085`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255625085/2026x+Running+server-side+simulation+with+SSL
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-12-03T10:13:14.396+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Server-side simulation > (2026x) Simulation using Jupyter Notebook
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

This chapter describes two ways to run server-side simulation with a self-signed certificate.

#### NOTE: Prerequisites

Prerequisites

- The self-signed certificate used for server-side simulation must be generated for the IP (or hostname) of the machine where Web Application Platform is located. This is necessary because Web Application Platform (and not Teamwork Cloud) is called when connecting to the server. Therefore, if Teamwork Cloud is deployed on a different server, do not use the same certificate for both Teamwork Cloud and Web Application Platform.
- The certificate file must be in the .pem format.

To run server-side simulation with SSL

- Do one of the following:
  - Use the following request to connect to Web Application Platform and provide the path to the certificate file: In the request, make sure to provide the IP or hostname for which the certificate was generated. ', '', '', verifySSL='')]]>
  - Add the self-signed certificate to the Python trusted certificates list by completing the following steps:
    1. In Jupyter Notebook, execute the following commands to find the path, where the *cacert.pem* file of trusted certificates is located:
    2. Open the certificate file with a text editor and copy all information inside the file.
    3. Open the *cacert.pem* file with a text editor and paste the copied information at the end of the file, as shown below. 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
    4. Use the following request to connect to Web Application Platform: In the request, make sure to provide the IP or hostname for which the certificate was generated. ', '', '', verifySSL=True)]]>

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>This chapter describes two ways to run server-side simulation with a self-signed certificate.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d10421a6-9839-4a9f-a8ad-00a71d06b036"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Prerequisites</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li data-uuid="e0062452-1f92-4b95-a51c-7d7e991488ee">The self-signed certificate used for server-side simulation must be generated for the IP (or hostname) of the machine where Web Application Platform is located. This is necessary because Web Application Platform (and not Teamwork Cloud) is called when connecting to the server. Therefore, if Teamwork Cloud is deployed on a different server, do not use the same certificate for both Teamwork Cloud and Web Application Platform.</li><li data-uuid="a8b392b3-eb50-43db-a128-0b82b65066f7">The certificate file must be in the <em>.pem</em> format.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p>To run server-side simulation with SSL</p><hr /><ul><li data-uuid="808536a8-f068-4e92-b6aa-cf8d1e609092">Do one of the following:<ul><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Use the following request to connect to Web Application Platform and provide the path to the certificate file:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="770c82a9-1454-4d9e-b060-2e8228acee95"><ac:rich-text-body><p>In the request, make sure to provide the IP or hostname for which the certificate was generated.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="8c1bcaa5-4c7c-45c1-b16b-7c6951a4951d"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[client = SimulationWebClient('<WebApp_server_IP_or_hostname>', '<user_name>', '<password>', verifySSL='<path_to_certificate_file>')]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li><li>Add the self-signed certificate to the Python trusted certificates list by completing the following steps:<ol><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">In Jupyter Notebook, execute the following commands to find the path, where the <em><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">cacert.pem</span></em> file of trusted certificates is located:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="44470736-2fb7-4956-8283-56c858d6f6c2"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[import certifi
print(certifi.where())]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Open the certificate file with a text editor and copy all information inside the file.</span></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Open the <em>cacert.pem</em> file with a text editor and paste the copied information at the end of the file, as shown below.<br /><ac:image ac:border="true" ac:height="691" ac:width="1076"><ri:attachment ri:filename="cacert_file.png" /></ac:image><br /></span></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Use the following request to connect to Web Application Platform:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="7d4904d8-d376-4cd3-ac5d-8ef42c2d3da0"><ac:rich-text-body><p>In the request, make sure to provide the IP or hostname for which the certificate was generated.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f7fc4737-7060-482a-9c78-b7a224fe241e"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[client = SimulationWebClient('WebApp_server_IP_or_hostname>', '<user_name>', '<password>', verifySSL=True)]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li></ol></li></ul></li></ul>
````
