<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255626370 space=MMA version=2 -->
## PAGE 00443: (2026x) Sample project

- page_id: `255626370`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255626370/2026x+Sample+project
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:47:28.511+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Parametric evaluator
- labels: ['parametric-simulation-samples']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The Parametric Simulation sample projects are available in the *<*install_root*>/samples/simulation/Parametrics* directory. The SysML Parametric diagrams and InstanceSpecifications are as follows

- simple_parametrics.mdzip: basic Parametric Simulation.
- The CylinderPipe.mdzip sample: demonstrates how to deal with multiple values. It shows the calculation for the cost of raw materials that will be used to manufacture the cylinder pipes. It also demonstrates the use of OpaqueBehaviorAction to simulate the parametric.
- The ActParIntegrate.mdzip sample: demonstrates the use of OpaqueBehavior to simulate the parametric.
- SCARA manipulator.mdzip: demonstrate the use of Parametric Simulation to evaluate the position of end-effector of the SCARA manipulator from the given angles of actuators.
- MotionAnalysis.mdzip and SpringDisplacementUsingTimevariable.mdzip: show how to use Time Series Chart for plotting the runtime values.
- Trade-Study for Brayton Cycle.mdzip: this sample shows you how to perform a trade-study.
- Forward Contract Valuation.mdzip: a dynamic constraint usage in Parametric Simulation.

#### NOTE: Note

Note

All of the sample projects of the Parametric Simulation engine include a Simulation Configurations package that contains two SimulationConfig elements for normal and silent simulation. You can select this SimulationConfig class to start the Parametric Simulation engine.

**Related pages**

- Specifying the language for the expression
- Value binding
- Evaluating expressions
- Evaluation with causality
- Dynamic constraint
- Manual value updates using the Parametric Evaluator
- Communicating with evaluators through simulation console
- Built-in Math
- Integration with external Evaluators
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Trade study analysis' space='']
- Sample project

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

The SysML Parametric diagrams and InstanceSpecifications are as follows</p><ul><li>simple_parametrics.mdzip: basic Parametric Simulation.</li><li>The CylinderPipe.mdzip sample: demonstrates how to deal with multiple values. It shows the calculation for the cost of raw materials that will be used to manufacture the cylinder pipes. <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255626192 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00444: (2026x) Sample projects

- page_id: `255626192`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255626192/2026x+Sample+projects
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:43:34.015+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) State Machine simulation
- labels: ['sample-projects', 'state-machine-simulation-samples']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

103619828

103619839

103619826

**On this page**

44

103619829

The State Machine simulation sample projects are available in the built-in **Small Tests**samples, in *<modeling_tool_installation_directory>\samples\simulation\SmallTestSamples.mdzip* or from the Welcome screen under the **Simulation** group of the modeling tool. In this topic, the focus is on the **State Tests** section for the State Machine simulation sample projects as follows:

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The State Tests section of the Small Tests samples.

- Do Activity in State This test is designed to demonstrate how to use the Do Behavior in model execution. Run the **TestState_DoActivity** simulation configuration to see how it works. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] The Do Activity in State sample.
- Guard This test is designed to demonstrate how to use Guards on Transitions in model execution. Run the **TestState_Guard** simulation configuration to see how it works. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] The Guard sample.
- History This test is designed to demonstrate how to use the Composite State with Shallow History and Deep History in model execution. Click the **Run** button on the diagram toolbar to see how it works. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] The History sample.
- Regions This test is designed to demonstrate how to use the Orthogonal State with Parallel Regions and Entry and Exit Activities in model execution. The Entry Activity is executed immediately after the State Machine is activated before any States in the inner regions. Next, all Initial States in the regions are activated simultaneously. The **Regions** sample demonstrates that multiple active States are executed at the same time. The Event list in the Simulation Console contains Triggers of all outgoing Transitions of all active States. If one of the parent States’ outgoing Transitions is triggered, the Exit Activity will be executed before the State Machine is deactivated. Click the **Run with Context** button on the diagram toolbar to see how it works. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] The Regions sample.
- Timing Events This test is designed to demonstrate how to use timing Events in model execution. Both relative time and absolute time are supported (**Is Relative=true/false**). Click the **Run** button on the diagram toolbar to see how it works. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] The Timing Events sample.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="3004aa07-cfa7-4ecd-9965-7f72f6acef23"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">103619828</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="7184c770-1b47-4d4a-8ee5-f5c7bf845ab3"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">103619839</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="7293f9fb-c8de-4f6a-be39-78cb746749d5"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">103619826</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>On this page</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="toc" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="8cdf6f6a-95a4-4f3f-936f-bdd0d97ba3c4"><ac:parameter ac:name="maxLevel">4</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="minLevel">4</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="8e6e8bcf-0613-4a42-9bf3-2ab7e39c66a2"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">103619829</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>The State Machine simulation sample projects are available in the built-in <strong>Small Tests </strong>samples,  in <em>&lt;modeling_tool_installation_directory&gt;\samples\simulation\SmallTestSamples.mdzip</em> or from the Welcome screen under the <strong>Simulation</strong> group of the modeling tool. In this topic, the focus is on the <strong>State Tests</strong> section for the State Machine simulation sample projects as follows:</p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="State Tests ฆection of the Small Test Sample.PNG"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Sample projects" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">The State Tests section of the Small Tests samples.</h6><ul><li><h4>Do Activity in State</h4><p>This test is designed to demonstrate how to use the Do Behavior in model execution. Run the <strong>TestState_DoActivity</strong> simulation configuration to see how it works.</p><h4 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Do Activity in State Sample.PNG"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Sample projects" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h4><h6 style="text-align: center;">The Do Activity in State sample.</h6></li><li><h4>Guard</h4><p>This test is designed to demonstrate how to use Guards on Transitions in model execution. Run the <strong>TestState_Guard</strong> simulation configuration to see how it works.</p><h4 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Guard Sample.PNG"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Sample projects" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h4><h6 style="text-align: center;">The Guard sample.</h6></li><li><h4>History</h4><p>This test is designed to demonstrate how to use the Composite State with Shallow History and Deep History in model execution. Click the <strong>Run</strong> button on the diagram toolbar to see how it works.</p><h4 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="History Sample.PNG"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Sample projects" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h4><h6 style="text-align: center;">The History sample.</h6></li><li><h4>Regions</h4><p>This test is designed to demonstrate how to use the Orthogonal State with Parallel Regions and Entry and Exit Activities in model execution. The Entry Activity is executed immediately after the State Machine is activated before any States in the inner regions. Next, all Initial States in the regions are activated simultaneously. The <strong>Regions</strong> sample demonstrates that multiple active States are executed at the same time. The Event list in the Simulation Console contains Triggers of all outgoing Transitions of all active States. If one of the parent States’ outgoing Transitions is triggered, the Exit Activity will be executed before the State Machine is deactivated. Click the <strong>Run with Context</strong> button on the diagram toolbar to see how it works.</p><h4 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Regions Sample.PNG"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Sample projects" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h4><h6 style="text-align: center;">The Regions sample.</h6></li><li><h4>Timing Events</h4><p>This test is designed to demonstrate how to use timing Events in model execution. Both relative time and absolute time are supported (<strong>Is Relative=true/false</strong>). Click the <strong>Run</strong> button on the diagram toolbar to see how it works.</p><h4 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Timing Events Sample.PNG"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Sample projects" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h4><h6 style="text-align: center;">The Timing Events sample.</h6></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255625056 space=MMA version=3 -->
## PAGE 00445: (2026x) Sample projects for simulation with Jupyter Notebook

- page_id: `255625056`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255625056/2026x+Sample+projects+for+simulation+with+Jupyter+Notebook
- version_number: 3
- version_date: `2025-12-03T10:14:40.732+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Server-side simulation > (2026x) Simulation using Jupyter Notebook
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Once you set up Jupyter Notebook, you can open and test sample projects created for server-side simulation using Jupyter. This chapter briefly describes the sample projects and explains how to execute them.

To access the sample projects

1. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Simulation using Jupyter Notebook' space=''] if you have not already done so.
2. Extract the < install_root >\plugins\com.nomagic.magicdraw.simulation\pyST.zip file. The sample projects are located in the samples directory.

##### Bouncing Ball sample

The *Bouncing Ball* sample project demonstrates server-side simulation with FMU. In addition, you can draw a chart based on the CSV data after the model execution.

#### NOTE: Prerequisites

Prerequisites

Add the Bouncing Ball sample project to the Teamwork Cloud server.

To run the *Bouncing Ball* sample and draw a chart based on the CSV data

1. Run the *Bouncing Ball* project and get the CSV data. Request example for running the simulation
2. Draw a chart based on the CSV data. Request example for drawing a chart based on the CSV data

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The *Bouncing Ball* chart drawn based on the CSV data.

##### Coffee Machine sample

The *Coffee Machine* sample project demonstrates server-side simulation with a specified HTML UI mockup. With this project, you can open the HTML UI window during model execution.

#### NOTE: Prerequisites

Prerequisites

- Add the Coffee Machine sample project to the Teamwork Cloud server.
- To open the HTML UI during server-side simulation, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Auto-generating HTML files' space=''] and [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Committing changes to Teamwork Cloud' space='MT'] .

To run the *Coffee Machine* sample and open the HTML UI window

1. Run the *Coffee Machine* project. Request example for running the simulation
2. Check the simulation status. Request example for getting the simulation status/simulation/api/ui/20a84d37-0aba-4e12-9f14-ca381a5952f4/CoffeeMachine.html'],
 'project': 'CoffeeMachine',
 'config': 'Coffee Machine Web',
 'elapsedTime': 72742}]]>
3. Click the UI URL in the response to the status request to open the HTML UI window.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The HTML UI window opened during the *Coffee Machine* project execution.

##### Spacecraft Mass Rollup sample

The *Spacecraft Mass Rollup* sample demonstrates server-side simulation with specified input and output parameters.

#### NOTE: Prerequisites

Prerequisites

Add the Spacecraft Mass Rollup sample project to the Teamwork Cloud server.

To run the *Spacecraft Mass Rollup* sample with input and output parameters

1. Do one or both of the following:
  - Specify a set of input parameters with values to be provided for the simulation.
  - Specify a set of output parameters to be obtained after the simulation is complete. If no output parameters are specified, all initialized values are returned. Request example for running the simulation with input and output parameters
2. Get the simulation results. Request example for getting simulation results

##### Car Braking Analysis sample

The *Car Braking Analysis* sample project demonstrates the server-side simulation that calculates stopping distance. You can use this project for the following scenarios:

- Seeing the relationship between the vehicle mass and braking distance.
- Calculating the stopping distance according to the car mass and speed.

#### NOTE: Prerequisites

Prerequisites

Car Braking Analysis

To run the *Car Braking Analysis* sample to see the relationship between the vehicle mass and braking distance

1. Run the *Car Braking Analysis* project while changing the mass of the car, e.g., by 20 kg. Request example for running the simulation with a changed mass
2. Draw a chart of the relationship between the vehicle mass and braking distance. Request example for drawing the chart

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The chart showing the relationship between the vehicle mass and stopping distance.

To run the *Car Braking Analysis* sample to calculate the stopping distance

1. Run the *Car Braking Analysis* sample to calculate the stopping distance according to the specified car mass and speed. Request example for running the simulation to calculate the stopping distance
2. Draw a chart to show the car stopping distance with the threshold of the required maximum stopping distance. Request example for drawing the chart

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The chart showing the car stopping distance with the threshold of the required maximum stopping distance.

##### Hinge Monte Carlo Analysis sample

The Hinge Monte Carlo sample project demonstrates a server-side simulation that runs the Monte Carlo analysis. In addition, you can draw a histogram chart based on the CSV data after the model execution.

#### NOTE: Prerequisite

Prerequisite

Add the *Hinge Monte Carlo Analysis* sample project to the Teamwork Cloud server.

To run the*Hinge Monte Carlo Analysis* and draw a histogram based on CSV data

1. Run the*Hinge Monte Carlo Analysis*and get the CSV data. Request example for running the simulation
2. Draw a histogrambased on the CSV data. Request example for drawing a histogram based on the CSV data

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The*Hinge Clearance Histogram*drawn based on the CSV data.

##### Cruise Control Widgets sample

The Cruise Control Widgets sample project demonstrates server-side simulation with a specified HTML UI mockup that includes widgets. With this project, you can open the HTML UI window during model execution.

#### NOTE: Prerequisite

Prerequisite

- Add the Cruise Control Widgets sample project to the Teamwork Cloud server.
- To open the HTML UI during server-side simulation, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Auto-generating HTML files' space=''] and [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Committing changes to Teamwork Cloud' space='MT'] .

To run the*Cruise Control Widgets*project and get the status of the specified simulation

1. Run the *Cruise Control Widgets*project. Request example for running the simulation
2. To get the status of the specified simulation. Request example for getting the status of the specified simulation/simulation/api/ui/160e822a-5c22-4758-9f1d-d72a83351559/SimulationWithWidgets.html'],
 'project': 'CruiseControl_Widgets',
 'config': 'Cruise Control with Widgets',
 'elapsedTime': 1657}]]>

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']The HTML UI window opened during the Cruise Control Widgets project execution.

**Sample projects**

- [ATTACHMENT filename='BouncingBall.ipynb']
- [ATTACHMENT filename='CoffeeMachine.ipynb']
- [ATTACHMENT filename='SpacecraftMassRollup.ipynb']
- [ATTACHMENT filename='CarBrakingAnalysis.ipynb']
- [ATTACHMENT filename='HingeMonteCarloAnalysis.ipynb']
- [ATTACHMENT filename='CruiseControlWidgets.ipynb']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p>Once you set up Jupyter Notebook, you can open and test sample projects created for server-side simulation using Jupyter. This chapter briefly describes the sample projects and explains how to execute them.</p><p><br /></p><p>To access the sample projects</p><hr /><ol><li><ac:link ac:anchor="Setting up Jupyter Notebook"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Simulation using Jupyter Notebook" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Set up Jupyter Notebook]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> if you have not already done so.</li><li>Extract the &lt;<em style="text-align: left;">install_root</em><em style="text-align: left;">&gt;\plugins\com.nomagic.magicdraw.simulation\pyST.zip</em> file. The sample projects are located in the <em>samples</em> directory.</li></ol><h3>Bouncing Ball sample</h3><p style="text-align: left;">The <em>Bouncing Ball</em> sample project demonstrates server-side simulation with FMU. In addition, you can draw a chart based on the CSV data after the model execution. </p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6f9e22db-5375-4835-9560-0e4f7ed34add"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Prerequisites</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body>Add the Bouncing Ball sample project to the Teamwork Cloud server.</ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p class="auto-cursor-target">To run the <em>Bouncing Ball</em> sample and draw a chart based on the CSV data</p><hr /><ol><li><p>Run the <em>Bouncing Ball</em> project and get the CSV data.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="450a45e1-0ee3-4711-9879-211e08462766"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Request example for running the simulation</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[result = client.simulate('BouncingBall", config='Run BouncingBall')
csv = result['csvExports']['Bouncing Ball']]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li><li><p>Draw a chart based on the CSV data.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="9f9f724e-cdb9-45d8-b14d-1f22da433201"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Request example for drawing a chart based on the CSV data</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot import figure
from io import StringIO

figure(figsize=(16, 8), dpi=80
x = np.loadtxt(StringIO(csv), dtype='double', delimiter=',', skiprows = 1, usecols = (0)) / 1000
y = np.loadtxt(StringIO(csv), dtype='double', delimiter=',', skiprows = 1, usecols = (1))
plt.xlabel('time (ms)')
plt.ylabel('height')
plt.title('Bouncing Ball', fontweight="bold", fontsize=20)
plt.plot(x, y,)
plt.grid()
plt.show()]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li></ol><p><br /></p><p class="auto-cursor-target" style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="bouncing_ball_chart.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 class="auto-cursor-target" style="text-align: center;">The <em>Bouncing Ball</em> chart drawn based on the CSV data.</h6><h3><br />Coffee Machine sample</h3><p style="text-align: left;">The <em>Coffee Machine</em> sample project demonstrates server-side simulation with a specified HTML UI mockup. With this project, you can open the HTML UI window during model execution.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e57371f4-34bc-43bc-b4a8-b4611cb39bd1"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Prerequisites</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>Add the <em>Coffee Machine</em> sample project to the Teamwork Cloud server.</li><li>To open the HTML UI during server-side simulation, <ac:link ac:anchor="Generating and attaching HTML files to Teamwork Cloud projects"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Auto-generating HTML files" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[attach the HTML UI to the server project]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> and <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MT" ri:content-title="Committing changes to Teamwork Cloud" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[commit the changes to the server]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p>To run the <em>Coffee Machine</em> sample and open the HTML UI window</p><hr /><ol><li><p>Run the <em>Coffee Machine</em> project.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="7920f543-c4e6-46c8-b203-dcca01e368b7"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Request example for running the simulation</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[client.run('CoffeeMachine', config='Coffee Machine Web')

{'state': 'INITIALIZING',
 'simulationId': '20a84d37-0aba-4e12-9f14-ca381a5952f4',
 'project': 'CoffeeMachine',
 'elapsedTime': 106}]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li><li><p>Check the simulation status.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e1d6ea40-afbe-4c41-ab51-1e6e3f6ba55b"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Request example for getting the simulation status</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[client.get_status('20a84d37-0aba-4e12-9f14-ca381a5952f4')

{'state': 'RUNNING',
 'simulationId': '20a84d37-0aba-4e12-9f14-ca381a5952f4',
 'simulationTime': '0 ms',
 'ui': ['<server_address>/simulation/api/ui/20a84d37-0aba-4e12-9f14-ca381a5952f4/CoffeeMachine.html'],
 'project': 'CoffeeMachine',
 'config': 'Coffee Machine Web',
 'elapsedTime': 72742}]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li><li><p>Click the UI URL in the response to the status request to open the HTML UI window.</p></li></ol><p><br /></p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="coffeemachine_UI.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">The HTML UI window opened during the <em>Coffee Machine</em> project execution.</h6><h3><br />Spacecraft Mass Rollup sample</h3><p style="text-align: left;">The <em>Spacecraft Mass Rollup</em> sample demonstrates server-side simulation with specified input and output parameters.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="7be87388-c7c1-4983-b705-e7dd874544a3"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Prerequisites</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body>Add the Spacecraft Mass Rollup sample project to the Teamwork Cloud server.</ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p>To run the <em>Spacecraft Mass Rollup</em> sample with input and output parameters</p><hr /><ol><li>Do one or both of the following:<ul><li>Specify a set of input parameters with values to be provided for the simulation.</li><li><p>Specify a set of output parameters to be obtained after the simulation is complete. If no output parameters are specified, all initialized values are returned.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a94da246-c32d-4507-afc1-531f0b4ec0c2"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Request example for running the simulation with input and output parameters</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[parameters = {
 "inputs":
   {
        "propulsion.thruster.me":32
        "telecom.amplifier.me":15
   },
 "outputs":
    [
        "me",
        "propulsion.me",
        "propulsion.tank.me",
        "propulsion.thruster.me",
        "telecom.me",
        "telecom.antenna.me",
        "telecom.amplifier.me"
    ]
}

client.run('SpacecraftMassRollup', config='spacecraft mass analysis', data=json.dumps(parameters))

{'state': 'INITIALIZING',
 'simulationId': 'fc5fa7eb-761a-4d31-9201-2f51f1754675',
 'project': 'SpacecraftMassRollup',
 'elapsedTime': 187}]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li></ul></li><li><p>Get the simulation results.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0a6b83af-2f64-47ca-9ab0-864be104dae4"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Request example for getting simulation results</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[client.get_result('fc5fa7eb-761a-4d31-9201-2f51f1754675')

{'outputs': {'me': 104.0,
  'telecom.me': 34.0,
  'telecom.amplifier.me': 15.0,
  'telecom.antenna.me': 19.0,
  'propulsion.me': 70.0,
  'propulsion.tank.me': 38.0
  'propulsion.thruster.me': 32.0},
 'verification': [{'property': 'propulsion.thruster.me',
   'status': 'fail',
   'value': 32.0,
   'requirements': [{'id': '1',
     'text': 'Estimated mass shall be less than allocated mass',
     'status': 'fail',
     'timestamp': 0}],
   'constraints': [{'constraint': 'me < ma',
     'status': 'fail',
     'timestamp': 0}]},
  {'property': 'telecom.amplifier.me',
   'status': 'fail',
   'value': 15.0,
   'requirements': [{'id': '1',
     'text': 'Estimated mass shall be less than allocated mass',
     'status': 'fail',
     'timestamp': 0}],
   'constraints': [{'constraint': 'me < ma',
     'status': 'fail',
     'timestamp': 0}]}]}]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li></ol><h3>Car Braking Analysis sample</h3><p style="text-align: left;">The <em>Car Braking Analysis</em> sample project demonstrates the server-side simulation that calculates stopping distance. You can use this project for the following scenarios:</p><ul><li>Seeing the relationship between the vehicle mass and braking distance.</li><li>Calculating the stopping distance according to the car mass and speed.</li></ul><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="76615562-f0b7-44b0-ad4a-e247f1be010d"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Prerequisites</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body>Add the <em>Car Braking Analysis</em> sample project to the Teamwork Cloud server.</ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p>To run the <em>Car Braking Analysis</em> sample to see the relationship between the vehicle mass and braking distance</p><hr /><ol><li><p>Run the <em>Car Braking Analysis</em> project while changing the mass of the car, e.g., by 20 kg.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="47e7284f-7619-400e-8a1a-53a74d3362de"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Request example for running the simulation with a changed mass</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[mass = [0] * 10
distance = [0] * 10
for x in range(0, 5):
    mass[x] = 800 + 20 * x
    parameters = {
      "inputs":
      {
        "grossMass": mass[x]
      }
    }
    results = client.simulate('CarBrakingAnalysis_final', config='Vehicle Analysis no Matlab', commit_results=False, data=json.dumps(parameters))
    distance[x] = results['outputs']['stoppingDistance']
    print(mass[x], distance[x] )]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li><li><p>Draw a chart of the relationship between the vehicle mass and braking distance.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5688e939-e2c9-4553-9a42-f68b097a0744"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Request example for drawing the chart</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[import numpty as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from matplotlib.pyplot import figure
from io import StringIO

figure(figsize=(16, 8), dpi=80)
plt.xlabel('mass (kg)')
plt.ylabel('stopping distance (m)')
plt.title('The relationship between vehicle mass and braking distance', fontweight="bold", fontsize=20)
plt.plot(mass, distance)
plt.grid()
plt.show()]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li></ol><p><br /></p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="vehicle_mass_vs_braking_distance.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">The chart showing the relationship between the vehicle mass and stopping distance.</h6><p><br /></p><p>To run the <em>Car Braking Analysis</em> sample to calculate the stopping distance</p><hr /><ol><li><p>Run the <em>Car Braking Analysis</em> sample to calculate the stopping distance according to the specified car mass and speed.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b6fde3de-37a4-404f-93fe-f2717838d8ed"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Request example for running the simulation to calculate the stopping distance</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[speed = input("Enter the car speed (km/h):")
totalMass = input("Enter the car mass (kg):")

parameters = {
  "inputs":
  {
    "grossMass": totalMass,
    "speed": speed
  }
}

results = client.simulate('CarBakingAnalysis', config='Vehicle Analysis no Matlab', data=json.dumps(parameters))
distance = results['outputs']["stoppingDistance"]
requiredDistance = results['outputs']["requiredStoppingDistance"]
print("Stopping distance:", distance)
print("Failed requirements:")
print(json.dumps(results['verification'], indent=2))]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li><li><p>Draw a chart to show the car stopping distance with the threshold of the required maximum stopping distance.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f47443bd-7452-479b-b73b-9e4b91885b32"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Request example for drawing the chart</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib as mpl
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

data = pd.DataFrame(
    {"distance": [distance]})
ax = data.plot(kind='bar'
              figsize=(10,7), color=['dodgerblue'], fontsize=13);
plt.axhline(y=requiredDistance, linewidth=3, linestyle='--', color='r')
ax.set_ylim([0, distance+50])
plt.title("car Braking Analysis Chart", fontsize=18, weight="bold")
plt.ylabel("Metre (m)", fontsize=15)
plt.text(0, distance+2, round(distance, 4), ha = 'center', fontsize=14)
ax.get_legend().remove()
plt.xticks(np.arange(1), ["Stopping distance"], rotation="horizontal", fontsize=15);]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li></ol><p><br /></p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="stopping_distance.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">The chart showing the car stopping distance with the threshold of the required maximum stopping distance.</h6><h3>Hinge Monte Carlo Analysis sample</h3><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">The Hinge Monte Carlo sample project demonstrates a server-side simulation that runs the Monte Carlo analysis. In addition, you can draw a histogram chart based on the CSV data after the model execution.</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="538933e4-c5a5-44a3-bfb3-8fd4bd9108bb"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Prerequisite</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Add the <em>Hinge Monte Carlo Analysis</em> sample project to the Teamwork Cloud server.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">To run the </span><em>Hinge Monte Carlo Analysis</em> <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">and draw a histogram based on CSV data</span></p><hr /><ol><li><p style="text-align: left;">Run the<span> <em>Hinge Monte Carlo Analysis</em> </span>and get the CSV data.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="1528fd86-b448-4105-a45d-214bd41ee5ae"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Request example for running the simulation</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[result = client.simulate('HingeMonteCarloAnalysis_Jupyter', config='Monte Carlo Analysis')
csv = result['csvExports']['clearance']]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li><li><p style="text-align: left;">Draw a <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">histogram </span>based on the CSV data.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f79f78d3-b71f-406d-bcf9-821c40b3c5fc"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Request example for drawing a histogram based on the CSV data</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
from matplotlib.pyplot import figure
from io import StringIO

x = np.loadtxt(StringIO(csv), dtype='double', delimiter=',', skiprows = 1, usecols = (0))
numberOfBins = 30

figure(figsize=(16, 8), dpi=80)
plt.hist(x, bins = numberOfBins)
plt.xlabel('hinge.clearance')
plt.ylabel('Frequency')
plt.title('Hinge Clearance Histogram', fontweight="bold", fontsize=20)
plt.xticks(np.arange(min(x), max(x), 0.5))
plt.grid()
plt.show()]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li></ol><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Histogram.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 class="auto-cursor-target" style="text-align: center;">The<span> </span><em>Hinge Clearance Histogram </em>drawn based on the CSV data.</h6><h3>Cruise Control Widgets sample</h3><p>The Cruise Control Widgets sample project demonstrates server-side simulation with a specified HTML UI mockup that includes widgets. With this project, you can open the HTML UI window during model execution.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d77c2833-7437-4b38-8402-4cfd760ba261"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Prerequisite</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>Add the <em>Cruise Control Widgets</em> sample project to the Teamwork Cloud server.</li><li>To open the HTML UI during server-side simulation, <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Auto-generating HTML files" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[attach the HTML UI to the server project]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> and <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MT" ri:content-title="Committing changes to Teamwork Cloud" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[commit the changes to the server]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">To run the </span><em>Cruise Control Widgets </em>project <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">and get the status of the specified simulation</span></p><hr /><ol><li><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">Run the <em>Cruise Control Widgets </em>project.<br /></span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b1087c00-6f1a-4869-a881-08b239df94e0"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Request example for running the simulation</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[result = client.run('CruiseControl_Widgets', config='Cruise Control with Widgets')
simID = result['simulationId']]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li><li><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">To get the status of the specified simulation.<br /></span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="779e4536-7465-42a9-b731-8a53680197eb"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Request example for getting the status of the specified simulation</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[client.get_status(simID)

{'state': 'RUNNING',
 'simulationId': '160e822a-5c22-4758-9f1d-d72a83351559',
'simulationTime': '0 ms',
 'ui': ['<server_address>/simulation/api/ui/160e822a-5c22-4758-9f1d-d72a83351559/SimulationWithWidgets.html'],
 'project': 'CruiseControl_Widgets',
 'config': 'Cruise Control with Widgets',
 'elapsedTime': 1657}]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li></ol><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:width="1200"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Cruise_Control_Sample.png" /></ac:image>The HTML UI window opened during the Cruise Control Widgets project execution.</h6></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>Sample projects</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:attachment ri:filename="BouncingBall.ipynb" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:attachment ri:filename="CoffeeMachine.ipynb" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:attachment ri:filename="SpacecraftMassRollup.ipynb" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:attachment ri:filename="CarBrakingAnalysis.ipynb" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:attachment ri:filename="HingeMonteCarloAnalysis.ipynb" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:attachment ri:filename="CruiseControlWidgets.ipynb" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255626056 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00446: (2026x) Saving plot results to the model

- page_id: `255626056`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255626056/2026x+Saving+plot+results+to+the+model
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:43:28.044+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Simulation Configuration and UI modeling > (2026x) Time Series Chart
- labels: ['plot-results', 'saving-plot-results']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Note

1417988244

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Note

1417988246

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Note

1417988245

The parameter **Result Location** in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) SimulationConfig stereotype' space='']<<SimulationConfig>> allows you to record and save plot results on a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Time Series Chart' space=''] into the model when simulation is completed. You can save the plot results in the**Result Location**parameterin the following formats: PNG file, CSV, or HTML file by using the **Record Plot Data As** option in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='MT'] of the Time series chart. The feature works with the Time series chart both stand-alone and in the UI diagram. You can then include the plot results into a generated document for reporting purposes.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Saving plot results using resultLocation in the <<SimulationConfig>>.

To save plot results using **Result Location**

1. Open the Specification window of a <<SimulationConfig>> and set **Result Location** to an InstanceSpecification.
2. Open the Specification window of a TimeSeriesChart and set **Record Plot Data As** to the PNG, CSV, or HTML format.
3. **Run** the <<SimulationConfig>> until it terminates.****The expected plot result will be saved into the InstanceSpecification. You can review the result through specification of the InstanceSpecification.

#### NOTE: Note

Note

- If Result Location is set to a package, the plot results will be saved into a new InstanceSpecification at each runtime. However, if Result Location is set to an InstanceSpecification, the results will be saved into the selected InstanceSpecification.
- After the simulation stopped, the plot results (both image and CSV values) will be saved in the Image and Comment properties of the specified InstanceSpecification if the Record Plot Data As option in [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Time Series Chart' space=''] is not specified.

The following figure shows the plot image saved in the Specification of Instance Specification of the bouncingBall model

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The plot image saved in the Image property of the specified InstanceSpecification of the bouncingBall model.

The image is too large to show in the **Image** property in the Specification window; therefore, you can verify it by dragging the InstanceSpecification into the diagram.

To verify a plot image saved in the Image property of the selected InstancedSpecification

1. Drag the InstanceSpecification to the diagram.
2. click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] on the top left of the InstanceSpecification. The **Compartments** shortcut menu will open. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] The Compartments menu icon on the top left of the InstanceSpecification. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
3. Select Stereotypes > Shape Image .

The following figure shows the CSV values saved in the Specification of Instance Specification of the bouncingBall model

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The plot CSV values are saved in the Body of the Comment property of the specified InstanceSpecification of bouncingBall model.

You can also specify the **Record Plot Data As** option in the Time series chart to your preferred plot result format (PNG, CSV, or HTML). If PNG is selected, you can verify the saved plot image by dragging the InstanceSpecification to the diagram and click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] on the top left of the InstanceSpecification to open the **Compartments** shortcut menu and select **Stereotypes** > **Shape Image**.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Verifying the saved plot image of the InstanceSpecification via **Compartments** > **Stereotypes** > **Shape Image**.

If the **Record Plot Data As** option is HTML, the HTML source will be generated into the **Documentation/Hyperlinks** property group. You can customize the title, logo, font, and other layouts of the generated HTML source and use them in other reports.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### HTML generated into the Documentation/Hyperlinks field of the InstanceSpecification.

The generated HTML can be saved and opened with a browser as shown in the following figure

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The HTML files of the saved plot results (The Record Plot Data As option is HTML).

1417988243

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) SimulationConfig stereotype' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Time Series Chart' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='MT']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255625045 space=MMA version=2 -->
## PAGE 00447: (2026x) Server-side simulation

- page_id: `255625045`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255625045/2026x+Server-side+simulation
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-12-03T09:50:40.275+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

If you work with Teamwork Cloud projects, you can simulate them on the server without using a modeling tool. You can execute your models using REST API, or the Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud user interface (if there are Cameo Collaborator documents published from those models). You can also run your models using the Resources application and Python client (pyST.py).

The following demo shows the server-side simulation in action:

800450

##### System requirements

The following Web Application Platform system requirements apply to an environmentthat runs a single server-side simulation. If you set up an environment where you can run concurrent server-side simulations, the system requirements applicable for a single simulation must be adjusted accordingly.

Web Application Platform system requirements for the environment with server-side simulation:

- Simple non-iterative simulations:
  - 1-core CPU
  - 1GB of RAM
  - Sample projects: <install_root>\samples\simulation\SpacecraftMassRollup.mdzip <install_root>\samples\simulation\CoffeeMachine.mdzip
- Simple iterative simulations:
  - 2-core CPU
  - 1GB of RAM
  - Sample projects: <install_root>\samples\simulation\TradeStudyExamples.mdzip 
<install_root>\samples\simulation\ HingeMonteCarloAnalysis.mdzip
- Complex, time-consuming simulations:
  - 4-core CPU
  - 2GB of RAM
  - Sample project: [ATTACHMENT filename='ElectricityConsumptionAnalysis.mdzip']

##### Installation information

The server-side simulation service is one of many Web Application Platform services provided as a separate application. After successfully installing the server-side simulation service, the Simulation application is displayed on the welcome page of the Web Application Platform.Opening the application will redirect you to the Server-side Simulation Swagger page.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Web Application Platform welcome screen

The server-side simulation service is installed automatically alongside Web Application Platform while using Teamwork Cloud or Magic Collaboration Studio installer. If you install Web Application Platform on a separate machine and use the Advanced Install option, select the server-side simulation service during installation. To learn more, refer to [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Web Application Platform Installation on a separate machine' space='MCS'].

To install the Server-side simulation

1. Install Web Application Platform.
2. Open the < wap_*install_root*>\apache-tomcat\shared\conf \webappplatform.properties file and set the sim.license.checkout property to true .
3. Restart Web Application Platform.

##### Licensing information

The server-side simulation functionality only works with a floating Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit license managed by the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='DSLS installation and licensing' space='IL']or [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='FlexNet license server installation and licensing' space='IL'].

You can setup the license either via the webappplatform.propertiesfile or the Web Application Platform. You can also configure the remote FlexNet license server for Web Application Platform services. To learn more, click [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Advanced services configuration' space='MCS'].

1. In the Web Application Platform, select **Settings>** **Simulation Toolkit Web license**. To learn more about Setting application, refer to the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Settings application' space='MCS'] page.
2. Do one of the following:
  - Click on the FlexNet tab, enter the license server name and click Apply . [ATTACHMENT filename='FlexNet.png']
  - Click on the DSLS tab, select the Organization defined or Custom radio button, enter the license server name and click Apply . 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

#### NOTE: Concurrent simulations

Concurrent simulations

- If the license is set in the webappplatform.properties file, it cannot be configured via the Admin Console. To use the WebApp platform, remove the license entry from the properties file and restart the WebApp service.
- To be able to run concurrent simulations, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Deployment example for Teamwork Cloud with services on Kubernetes' space='MCS'] .
- When executing multiple simulations at the same time, every new concurrent simulation requires an additional Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit license.

##### Preparing projects for server-side simulation

To simulate a project on the server, it must meet the following criteria:

- A project has to be added to Teamwork Cloud.
- For simulation results to be saved, you need to specify the Result Location property of a Simulation Configuration.

To control the auto-starting and auto-terminating properties of server-side simulation, specify the**autoStart**and**autoTerminate**properties of SimulationConfig accordingly. To learn more, refer to[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) SimulationConfig stereotype' space=''].

##### Required permissions

To perform server-side simulation, you need to have the following Teamwork Cloud permissions:

- Read Resources - allows you to simulate resources.
- Edit Resources - allows you to commit the simulation results to a resource.
- **Manage Simulations** - allows you to manage all simulations, including the ability to get and review simulation results and terminate simulations executed by other users.

To manage all simulations, in addition to the Manage Simulations permission, you need to have the Read Resources permission for the related resources.

##### Server-side simulation limitations

There are several limitations related to server-side simulation:

- Math engine integrations (Matlab, Mathematica, etc.) are not available.
- FMU/FMU co-simulation with Windows binaries is not available.
- UseCase Execution is not available.
- DSL stereotypes are not applied to created Slot and InstanceSpecification elements.
- Finding elements by Name (e.g Signals, Operations with ALH, mapping with ExcelHelper) is not available.

**Related pages**

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p>If you work with Teamwork Cloud projects, you can simulate them on the server without using a modeling tool. You can execute your models using REST API, or the Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud user interface (if there are Cameo Collaborator documents published from those models). Y<span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">ou can also run your models using the Resources application and Python client (pyST.py).</span></p><p>The following demo shows the server-side simulation in action:<br /><br /></p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:structured-macro ac:name="widget" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e7b1611d-60ca-4165-a483-0dccb7f0b75d"><ac:parameter ac:name="width">800</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="url"><ri:url ri:value="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=B1-VA0u8TdU" /></ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="height">450</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p><h3>System requirements</h3><p style="text-align: left;">The following Web Application Platform system requirements apply to <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">an environment</span><span style="text-decoration: none;color:var(--ds-text-accent-purple-bolder,#352c63);"> that runs a single server-side simulation</span>. If you set up an environment where you can run concurrent server-side simulations, <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">the system requirements applicable for a single simulation must be adjusted accordingly</span>.</p><p style="text-align: left;">Web Application Platform system requirements for the environment with server-side simulation:</p><ul style="text-align: left;"><li>Simple non-iterative simulations:<br /><ul><li>1-core CPU</li><li>1GB of RAM</li><li>Sample projects:<br /><em>&lt;install_root&gt;\samples\simulation\SpacecraftMassRollup.mdzip<br /></em><em>&lt;install_root&gt;\samples\simulation\CoffeeMachine.mdzip</em></li></ul></li><li>Simple iterative simulations:<br /><ul><li>2-core CPU</li><li>1GB of RAM</li><li>Sample projects:<br /><em>&lt;install_root&gt;\samples\simulation\TradeStudyExamples.mdzip<br />&lt;install_root&gt;\samples\simulation\</em><em>HingeMonteCarloAnalysis.mdzip</em></li></ul></li><li>Complex, time-consuming simulations:<br /><ul><li>4-core CPU</li><li>2GB of RAM</li><li>Sample project: <em><ac:link><ri:attachment ri:filename="ElectricityConsumptionAnalysis.mdzip" /></ac:link></em></li></ul></li></ul><h3>Installation information</h3><p>The server-side simulation service is one of many Web Application Platform services provided as a separate application<span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">. <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">After successfully installing the server-side simulation service, the Simulation application is displayed on the welcome page of the Web Application Platform. </span>Opening the application will redirect you to the Server-side Simulation Swagger page.</span></p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="welcome_screen.png" /></ac:image></span></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Web Application Platform welcome screen</span></h6><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a84365c2-9f54-435e-9108-a8ee64bfc7b4"><ac:rich-text-body><p>The server-side simulation service is installed automatically alongside Web Application Platform while using Teamwork Cloud or Magic Collaboration Studio installer. If you install Web Application Platform on a separate machine and use the Advanced Install option, select the server-side simulation service during installation. To learn more, refer to <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCS" ri:content-title="Web Application Platform Installation on a separate machine" /></ac:link><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">. </span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p>To install the Server-side simulation</p><hr /><ol><li>Install Web Application Platform.</li><li>Open the &lt;<em>wap_<em style="text-align: left;">install_root</em>&gt;\apache-tomcat\shared\conf</em><em><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">\</span>webappplatform.properties</em> file and set the <strong>sim.license.checkout</strong> property to <em>true</em>.</li><li>Restart Web Application Platform.</li></ol><h3>Licensing information</h3><p>The server-side simulation functionality only works with a floating Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit license managed by the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="IL" ri:content-title="DSLS installation and licensing" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[ DSLS ]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>or <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="IL" ri:content-title="FlexNet license server installation and licensing" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[FlexNet license server]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</p><p><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">You can setup the license either via the webappplatform.properties</span><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> file or the <span>Web Application Platform</span>. <span>You can also configure the remote FlexNet license server for Web Application Platform services. To learn more, click <ac:link ac:anchor="Configuring remote FlexNet license server for Web Application Platform services"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCS" ri:content-title="Advanced services configuration" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[here]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</span><span><br /></span></span></p><hr /><ol><li><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">In the <span>Web Application Platform, select <strong>Settings&gt;</strong></span> <strong>Simulation Toolkit Web license</strong>. To learn more about Setting application, refer to the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCS" ri:content-title="Settings application" /></ac:link> page.</span></li><li data-uuid="83a0c131-c681-4d2c-8910-1462e767c690">Do one of the following: <ul><li data-uuid="103de1da-ed96-4469-b03d-f29a29ab01b0">Click on the <strong>FlexNet</strong> tab, enter the license server name and click <strong>Apply</strong>.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:border="true" ac:height="133" ac:width="1007"><ri:attachment ri:filename="FlexNet.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li data-uuid="53569df2-e368-4d97-95c0-7930b3afa65d">Click on the <strong>DSLS </strong>tab, select the <strong>Organization defined </strong>or <strong>Custom </strong>radio button, enter the license server name and click <strong style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Apply</strong><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:border="true"><ri:attachment ri:filename="DSLS.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></span></li></ul></li></ol><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="55b8f3a9-abb0-41d4-adde-bd7d93333645"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Concurrent simulations</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li data-uuid="202b6813-0f5c-480e-92b7-0ae3d77cdee2">If the license is set in the webappplatform.properties file, it cannot be configured via the Admin Console. To use the WebApp platform, remove the license entry from the properties file and restart the WebApp service.</li><li>To be able to run concurrent simulations, <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCS" ri:content-title="Deployment example for Teamwork Cloud with services on Kubernetes" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[deploy the server-side simulation service by using Kubernetes and Docker]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</li><li>When executing multiple simulations at the same time, every new concurrent simulation requires an additional Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit license.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3>Preparing projects for server-side simulation</h3><p>To simulate a project on the server, it must meet the following criteria:</p><ul><li>A project has to be added to Teamwork Cloud.</li><li>For simulation results to be saved, you need to specify the <strong>Result Location</strong> property of a Simulation Configuration.</li></ul><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b4a8f404-f097-4c74-93bb-642e12dd0a8d"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">To control the auto-starting and auto-terminating properties of server-side simulation, specify the<span> </span></span><strong style="text-align: left;">autoStart</strong><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);"><span> </span>and<span> </span></span><strong style="text-align: left;">autoTerminate</strong><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);"><span> </span>properties of SimulationConfig accordingly. To learn more, refer to </span><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) SimulationConfig stereotype" /></ac:link><span>. </span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3>Required permissions</h3><p>To perform server-side simulation, you need to have the following Teamwork Cloud permissions:</p><ul style="text-align: left;"><li><strong>Read Resources</strong><span> </span>- allows you to simulate resources.</li><li><strong>Edit Resources</strong><span> </span>- allows you to commit the simulation results to a resource.</li><li><p><strong>Manage Simulations</strong> - <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">allows you to manage all simulations, including the ability to get and review simulation results and terminate simulations executed by other users.</span></p></li></ul><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="259d4ca8-5a3a-4eea-9988-3fdfe32ba813"><ac:rich-text-body><p>To manage all simulations, in addition to the Manage Simulations permission, you need to have the Read Resources permission for the related resources.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3>Server-side simulation limitations</h3><p>There are several limitations related to server-side simulation:</p><ul><li>Math engine integrations (Matlab, Mathematica, etc.) are not available.</li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">FMU/FMU co-simulation with Windows binaries is not available.</span></li><li>UseCase Execution is not available.</li><li>DSL stereotypes are not applied to created Slot and InstanceSpecification elements.</li><li>Finding elements by Name (e.g Signals, Operations with ALH, mapping with ExcelHelper) is not available.</li></ul></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="b014b16f-fb9b-4c6d-b8c1-28d5521e6125" /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255625157 space=MMA version=1 -->

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255625706 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00449: (2026x) Signal properties mapping to Behavior parameters

- page_id: `255625706`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255625706/2026x+Signal+properties+mapping+to+Behavior+parameters
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:42:58.199+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) State Machine simulation > (2026x) Adapting models for State Machine simulation
- labels: ['signal-property-mapping', 'mapping-signal-properties-to-behavior-parameters']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

119117224

119117227

119117225

Once the Activities Entry, Exit, and Do Activity of a State have been specified with a Behavior that has input parameters, a signal, which triggers objects to change the State, can carry values to the input parameters.

In order to send the signal to the target object, an instance of the signal will be created. The signal instance can contain the values of its structural features. These values will be propagated to the parameters of the Behavior specified at the Entry, Exit, and Do Activity when the two following conditions are met

- The number and order of properties of the signal must be the same as the number and order of parameters in the Behavior.
- The type, the order (isOrdered), and the multiplicity of each property of the signal must be the same as the corresponding parameter of the Behavior. If the type of signal properties are Subtype of the parameter type, it is considered a match.

If one of the two conditions is not satisfied, a warning will appear once the model is validated by pre-simulation constraints before the simulation starts.

A send signal Action in an Activity can create a signal instance with the values of its structural features. For example, the signal **MySignal** shown in the following figure. It contains three properties: attrib1, attrib2, and attrib3. They are created with Real, String, and Boolean respectively.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
A Signal with three properties.

The following figure shows the order of the three signal attributes

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
The arguments of MySignal signal.

To create the signal Instance of MySignal that contains the values of attrib1, attrib2 and attrib3, a send signal Action must be created with three argument pins as shown in the following figure. The order and types of the pins must match the order and type of the signal properties, therefore the first pin must be typed by Real, the second by String, and the third by Boolean.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Send signal Actions with three arguments.

Now that the pins of the send signal Action have been created, you can specify the values of the signal Instance through them (see the following figure). The figure shows the value specification Actions that are used to create the values and add them to the pins of the send signal Action.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Creating values for argument and parameters of MySignal.

At this point, the signal Instance created by the send signal Action in the **Send Signal Actions with three arguments** figure, will be sent to the context object of Activity A. The signal Instance contains **12.34**, **Hello** **World**, and **true** as the values of attrib1, attrib2, and attrib3 respectively. You can get more information on State Machine simulation and Activity simulation in sections [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) State Machine simulation' space=''] and [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Activity simulation' space=''] respectively.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Details of the State Machine diagram.

In a State-Machine simulation, the state of an object will change if it receives a send signal instance. Behaviors that are specified at the entry, do Activity, and exit of the State will be invoked in the State transition. The values that come with the signal instance will be delivered to the parameters of the behaviors. For example, prior to receiving a signal, an object is in State1, however, it will move to State2 once it has accepted the signal. The Behaviors to which the values of the signal Instance will be delivered, are the Behaviors specified at Exit of State1, Entry and Do Activity of State2.

In the following figure, the object has accepted the signal Instance, that is MySignal, and it will move from State1 to State2. A performOnEntry activity, which is the entry Activity of State 2 will be simulated. The performOnEntry Activity contains three parameters which are param1, param2, and param3. They are typed by Real, String, and Boolean respectively.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Details of the performOnEntry Activity.

You can see the order of the parameters. The values that come with the signal Instance of MySignal will be delivered to these parameters. In this example, the value of attrib1 will be propagated to param1, the value of attrib2 will be propagated to param2, and the value of attrib3 will be propagated to param3. The values of param1, param2, and param3 are "12.34", "Hello World", and "true" respectively. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Arguments of the performOnEntry Activity.

119117223

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) State Machine simulation' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Activity simulation' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5dc1e7ef-e486-4fef-85a2-4cfcd8d0987a"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">119117224</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="3abaca81-437d-4028-ab38-14f31f0a70d9"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">119117227</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0b3f5c44-97a6-4ffe-9df8-8e271eb5b1a4"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">119117225</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Once the Activities Entry, Exit, and Do Activity of a State have been specified with a Behavior that has input parameters, a signal, which triggers objects to change the State, can carry values to the input parameters. </p><p>In order to send the signal to the target object, an instance of the signal will be created. The signal instance can contain the values of its structural features. These values will be propagated to the parameters of the Behavior specified at the Entry, Exit, and Do Activity when the two following conditions are met</p><ul><li>The number and order of properties of the signal must be the same as the number and order of parameters in the Behavior. </li><li>The type, the order (isOrdered), and the multiplicity of each property of the signal must be the same as the corresponding parameter of the Behavior. If the type of signal properties are Subtype of the parameter type, it is considered a match. </li></ul><p>If one of the two conditions is not satisfied, a warning will appear once the model is validated by pre-simulation constraints before the simulation starts. </p><p>A send signal Action in an Activity can create a signal instance with the values of its structural features. For example, the signal <strong>MySignal</strong> shown in the following figure. It contains three properties: attrib1, attrib2, and attrib3. They are created with Real, String, and Boolean respectively. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="A Signal with Three Properties" ac:alt="A Signal with Three Properties"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Signal with 3 properties.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Signal properties mapping to Behavior parameters" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br />A Signal with three properties.</h6><p>The following figure shows the order of the three signal attributes</p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Arguments of MySignal Signal" ac:alt="Arguments of MySignal Signal"><ri:attachment ri:filename="specification-of-signal.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Signal properties mapping to Behavior parameters" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br />The arguments of MySignal signal.</h6><p>To create the signal Instance of MySignal that contains the values of attrib1, attrib2 and attrib3, a send signal Action must be created with three argument pins as shown in the following figure. The order and types of the pins must match the order and type of the signal properties, therefore the first pin must be typed by Real, the second by String, and the third by Boolean. </p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Send Signal Actions with Three Arguments" ac:alt="Send Signal Actions with Three Arguments"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Sending Signal Action with Arguments.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Signal properties mapping to Behavior parameters" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br />Send signal Actions with three arguments.<br class="atl-forced-newline" /> </h6><p>Now that the pins of the send signal Action have been created, you can specify the values of the signal Instance through them (see the following figure). The figure shows the value specification Actions that are used to create the values and add them to the pins of the send signal Action. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Creating Values for Argument and Parameters of MySignal" ac:alt="Creating Values for Argument and Parameters of MySignal"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Creating Values for Arguments and Parameters of MySignal.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Signal properties mapping to Behavior parameters" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br />Creating values for argument and parameters of MySignal.</h6><p>At this point, the signal Instance created by the send signal Action in the <strong>Send Signal Actions with three arguments</strong> figure, will be sent to the context object of Activity A. The signal Instance contains <strong>12.34</strong>, <strong>Hello</strong> <strong>World</strong>, and <strong>true</strong> as the values of attrib1, attrib2, and attrib3 respectively. You can get more information on State Machine simulation and Activity simulation in sections <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) State Machine simulation" /></ac:link> and <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Activity simulation" /></ac:link> respectively. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Details of Untitled1 Statemachine" ac:alt="Details of Untitled1 Statemachine"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Details of State Machine named Entitled1.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Signal properties mapping to Behavior parameters" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> <br />Details of the State Machine diagram.</h6><p>In a State-Machine simulation, the state of an object will change if it receives a send signal instance. Behaviors that are specified at the entry, do Activity, and exit of the State will be invoked in the State transition. The values that come with the signal instance will be delivered to the parameters of the behaviors. For example, prior to receiving a signal, an object is in State1, however, it will move to State2 once it has accepted the signal. The Behaviors to which the values of the signal Instance will be delivered, are the Behaviors specified at Exit of State1, Entry and Do Activity of State2. </p><p>In the following figure, the object has accepted the signal Instance, that is MySignal, and it will move from State1 to State2. A performOnEntry activity, which is the entry Activity of State 2 will be simulated. The performOnEntry Activity contains three parameters which are param1, param2, and param3. They are typed by Real, String, and Boolean respectively. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Details of performOnEntry Activity" ac:alt="Details of performOnEntry Activity"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Details of PerformOnEntry Activity.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Signal properties mapping to Behavior parameters" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br />Details of the performOnEntry Activity.</h6><p style="text-align: left;">You can see the order of the parameters. The values that come with the signal Instance of MySignal will be delivered to these parameters. In this example, the value of attrib1 will be propagated to param1, the value of attrib2 will be propagated to param2, and the value of attrib3 will be propagated to param3. The values of param1, param2, and param3 are &quot;12.34&quot;, &quot;Hello World&quot;, and &quot;true&quot; respectively. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Arguments of performOnEntry Activity" ac:alt="Arguments of performOnEntry Activity"><ri:attachment ri:filename="specification-of-activity-on-entry.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Signal properties mapping to Behavior parameters" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Arguments of the performOnEntry Activity.</h6></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="dd50a514-9b64-48ab-b96f-1111729d4aa4"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">119117223</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) State Machine simulation" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Activity simulation" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255625235 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00450: (2026x) Simulation Configuration and UI modeling

- page_id: `255625235`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255625235/2026x+Simulation+Configuration+and+UI+modeling
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:42:36.975+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide
- labels: ['simulation-configuration', 'ui-modeling', 'modeling-ui', 'user-interface']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

This chapter discusses the components as follows

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Project options' space=''] Setting options for a Simulation project, e.g., animation, simulation framework, and Simulation engines.
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) SimulationConfig stereotype' space=''] «SimulationConfig» and the SimulationConfig stereotypes as a model-based execution configuration through the «stereotype» SimulationConfig .
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Simulation log' space=''] Recording all event occurrences during simulation of a Simulation Configuration.
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Simulation time and simulation clock' space=''] Enabling Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit to obtain the amount of time spent on simulating a model from a simulation clock.
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Automatic start of active objects' space=''] An active object whose Classifier is an active Class.
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) UI modeling diagram simulation' space=''] UI components supporting the UI modeling diagram simulation.
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) ImageSwitcher and ActiveImage' space=''] ImageSwitcher as a powerful animation tool and «ActiveImage» attributes.
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Time Series Chart' space=''] Displaying plots of runtime values with respect to simulation time and its properties.
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Timeline chart' space=''] Allowing you to see the active States of an object or a property during simulation or the Activities simulated in an object.
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Histogram' space=''] Displaying the density of the underlying distribution of data and estimating the probability density function of underlying variables.
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Nested UI Configuration stereotype' space=''] Creating complex UI mockup consisting of multiple UI Configs.
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Reusable UI Mockup' space=''] Improving the flexibility in user interface modeling from typing the property using the UI component.
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) CSV export' space=''] Exporting simulation results to a CSV file.
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Nested property selection for configurations' space=''] Supporting nested property selection for configurations, e.g., Time series charts, Timelines, Sequence diagram generator, and CSV export.
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Attached files supported' space=''] Accessing files as «AttachedFile» by using only file names without paths in case of references to files by names.
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) HTML UI' space=''] Exporting, opening, and using HTML UI mockups and control model simulations within an embedded browser.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE
