````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>On this page</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="toc" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="06ba012f-efbe-40f6-8da1-afad85468bf4"><ac:parameter ac:name="maxLevel">4</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><h3>Calling a specific behavior</h3><p>You can use the following APIs to call any Behavior by its name or its qualified name.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ae1961a7-351c-4525-b6a4-406a8111050e"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[ALH.callBehavior("name")
ALH.callBehavior("name", List arguments)
ALH.callBehavior("name", List arguments, boolean isSynchronous)]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>The following example shows how to call the &quot;test&quot; specified Behavior in ALH API.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="71985e02-9d18-4522-bbc9-989acb3d20eb"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[value = ALH.callBehavior("test");]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>You can also give initial parameter values in which the argument list must be created.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="360254c4-2317-4ae9-911c-ee9364e495f9"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[arguments = ALH.createList();
arguments.add("value1");
arguments.add("value2");
value = ALH.callBehavior("test", arguments);]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>If a found Behavior value is null or an argument's size is not equal to the in or input parameter's size of the found Behavior, an IllegalArgumentException will be thrown.</p><h3>Calling a specific operation</h3><p>You can use the following APIs to call an operation by its name or its qualified name.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="54ba7e3f-6264-4a5d-94c1-bd483a995a27"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[ALH.callOperation("name")
ALH.callOperation("name", List arguments)
ALH.callOperation(object, "name")
ALH.callOperation(object, "name", List arguments)
ALH.callOperation(object, "name", List arguments, boolean isSynchronous)
ALH.callOperation(object, "name", "portName")
ALH.callOperation(object, "name", "portName", List arguments)
]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="42bbdb56-eb83-4ab2-bb12-f6653bf213c3"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span style="color: rgb(23,43,77);">If the object is omitted then context (self) is used automatically.</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>In the following example, you can learn how to call operation &quot;test&quot; and return its value to variable &quot;result&quot;.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="05a74076-308d-44aa-8e05-80b3ab61a463"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[result= ALH.callOperation("test");]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>In the following example, you can learn how to call a specified operation in ALH API by passing the operation's name and source port.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e79b5140-62be-471b-b4df-7fbd6bec7a27"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[ ALH.callOperation("start", "p1")]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>You can also give initial parameter values in which the arguments lists must be created by executing the following code.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ac9588b3-ae3d-4235-816f-dbb40dec694d"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[arguments = ALH.createList();
arguments.add("value1");
arguments.add("value2");
value = ALH.callOperation("test", arguments);]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>If a found Behavior value is null or an argument's size is not equal to the in or inout parameter's size of the found Behavior, an IllegalArgumentException will be thrown.</p><h3>Creating list of arguments</h3><p><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">A Java List will be required under certain circumstances, e.g., if you want to create Argument values that will be passed to some calling Behaviors or Operations</span><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">.</span></p><p>The following code fragment shows how to create the Java ArrayList in the scripting language with ALH:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="997b85ad-a038-4221-b8fc-fd5ceb07ee49"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[arguments = ALH.createList();
arguemnts.add(20);]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>Alternatively, you can use an initial value as the constructor's parameter.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e76c6594-b78a-44be-ba12-fec5bb9410a0"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[arguments = ALH.createList(20);]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="68a1b4cd-7d9a-418d-b6e1-f390e77d35de"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Note</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>Only one initial value of the list is allowed.</li><li>Variable-length argument lists are not supported.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249570264 space=MMA version=2 -->
## PAGE 00548: Carrying values using connectors

- page_id: `249570264`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249570264/Carrying+values+using+connectors
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-09-12T15:08:36.874+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Simulation debugging > Runtime Value Monitoring
- labels: ['carrying-values-via-connectors', 'connectors']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

43209831

43209833

43209832

A connector can carry a runtime value over from one connectable element to another if the following three conditions are present

- Datatypes on both elements are the same or compatible (equality of value can be defined), or one is the subtype of another.
- If the elements are nested features, the owner of the nested features must exist.
- If the elements are nested properties, in this example, b1, a connector must be drawn from a3 to the port and from the port to b1 because UML does not have a concrete definition about nested properties. However, SysML does have a definition about nested properties; therefore, the connector can be drawn between nested properties without the help of any ports.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Using Port and Connector to connect nested properties.

When primitive datatypes, in this example, n and a1, are bound together, the runtime values related to the role of both connector ends must be equal (but not necessarily the same instance). If the feature value of a data type value on one end changes, the data type value on the opposite end will change as well. As a result, n must equal a1, r must equal a2, s must equal a3, and b must equal a4 as follows.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Connectors connecting primitive datatypes.

43209830

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Connector' space='MT']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Binding Connector' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="1fb4fa66-28fc-43f5-8af4-7379de727518"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">43209831</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a60db43b-69a0-435c-a1df-5a8916fa058c"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">43209833</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="cf6126ca-8f73-482a-a747-c9ca0702cf27"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">43209832</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>A connector can carry a runtime value over from one connectable element to another if the following three conditions are present</p><ul><li>Datatypes on both elements are the same or compatible (equality of value can be defined), or one is the subtype of another. </li><li>If the elements are nested features, the owner of the nested features must exist. </li><li>If the elements are nested properties, in this example, b1, a connector must be drawn from a3 to the port and from the port to b1 because UML does not have a concrete definition about nested properties. However, SysML does have a definition about nested properties; therefore, the connector can be drawn between nested properties without the help of any ports. </li></ul><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Using Port and Connector to Connect Nested Properties" ac:alt="Using Port and Connector to Connect Nested Properties"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Using Port and Connector to Connect Nested Properties.png" /></ac:image><br />Using Port and Connector to connect nested properties.</h6><p>When primitive datatypes, in this example, n and a1, are bound together, the runtime values related to the role of both connector ends must be equal (but not necessarily the same instance). If the feature value of a data type value on one end changes, the data type value on the opposite end will change as well. As a result, n must equal a1, r must equal a2, s must equal a3, and b must equal a4 as follows. </p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Connectors Connecting Primitive Datatypes" ac:alt="Connectors Connecting Primitive Datatypes"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Connectors_connecting_primitive_datatypes.png" /></ac:image><br />Connectors connecting primitive datatypes.</h6></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0a07061d-ae39-40f2-9a0b-dc390e1391f7"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">43209830</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MT" ri:content-title="Connector" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Binding Connector" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249570873 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00549: Change Structural Feature Event

- page_id: `249570873`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249570873/Change+Structural+Feature+Event
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2018-02-19T10:27:36.214+01:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Simulation of SysML models > Supported SysML elements
- labels: ['change-structural-feature-event']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

A Change Structural Feature Event is an Event that occurs when a value of a specified Structural Feature on the Event changes. The Change Structural Feature Event will be accepted by an Accept Change Structural Feature Event action. You can find more information about simulation of an Accept Change Structural Feature Event Action and a Change Structural Feature Event in [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Accept Change Structural Feature Event Action' space=''].

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>A Change Structural Feature Event is an Event that occurs when a value of a specified Structural Feature on the Event changes. The Change Structural Feature Event will be accepted by an Accept Change Structural Feature Event action. You can find more information about simulation of an Accept Change Structural Feature Event Action and a Change Structural Feature Event in <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Accept Change Structural Feature Event Action" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Accept change structural feature Event action]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>. </p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249570530 space=MMA version=2 -->
## PAGE 00550: Changing Fork behavior

- page_id: `249570530`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249570530/Changing+Fork+behavior
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-19T14:56:13.119+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Activity simulation
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit allows you to specify whether Forks in Activity Diagrams run in a parallel or sequential way during simulation. You can change the behavior of an individual Fork by adding a constraint with a hardcoded keyword (*sequential* or *parallel*). If a Fork is set to run in parallel, all outgoing edges from that Fork run simultaneously. If a Fork is set to run sequentially, only one outgoing edge from that Fork can run at a time.

If you change the Fork behavior by adding constraints as described in this chapter, this behavior will override the **Run Forks In Parallel** property of a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SimulationConfig stereotype' space=''].

To change Fork behavior during simulation

- In an Activity Diagram, right-click a Fork and do one of the following: [ATTACHMENT filename='changing_fork_behavior.png']
  - If you want the fork to run sequentially, go to Simulation > Occurrence > Sequential .
  - If you want the fork to run in parallel, go to Simulation > Occurrence > Parallel .

After you change the behavior of a Fork as described above, the label indicating the specified behavior is added to the Fork symbol (either *sequential*, or *parallel*).

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit allows you to specify whether Forks in Activity Diagrams run in a parallel or sequential way during simulation. You can change the behavior of an individual Fork by adding a constraint with a hardcoded keyword (<em>sequential</em> or <em>parallel</em>). If a Fork is set to run in parallel, all outgoing edges from that Fork run simultaneously. If a Fork is set to run sequentially, only one outgoing edge from that Fork can run at a time. </p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0f7663df-f710-4098-8e71-f6081d9bb46f"><ac:rich-text-body><p>If you change the Fork behavior by adding constraints as described in this chapter, this behavior will override the <strong>Run Forks In Parallel</strong> property of a <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="SimulationConfig stereotype" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Simulation Configuration]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p>To change Fork behavior during simulation</p><hr /><ul><li>In an Activity Diagram, right-click a Fork and do one of the following:<ul><li>If you want the fork to run sequentially, go to <strong>Simulation</strong> &gt; <strong>Occurrence</strong> &gt; <strong>Sequential</strong>.</li><li>If you want the fork to run in parallel, go to <strong>Simulation</strong> &gt; <strong>Occurrence</strong> &gt; <strong>Parallel</strong>.<br /><br /></li></ul><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="changing_fork_behavior.png" /></ac:image></li></ul><p><br />After you change the behavior of a Fork as described above, the label indicating the specified behavior is added to the Fork symbol (either <em>sequential</em>, or <em>parallel</em>).</p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249571075 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00551: Checking Behaviors

- page_id: `249571075`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249571075/Checking+Behaviors
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-04-04T14:14:31.522+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Action languages > Action scripts APIs > Manipulating runtime objects using scripts
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**On this page**

4

##### Getting the current State of a runtime object

You can use the following ALH API to get the current State of a specified object. If the object has more than one active State, it returns the first active state. The API returns the instance of the state.

```text

```

The following code fragment shows how to get the current state of a specified object using ALH API:

```text

```

##### Checking the State of an object

You can use script to check state of an object. You can use the fUML object syntax, with **object.in("stateName")***.* For example:

- self.in("Operating");

Alternatively, you can check the state of an object using ALH API. The API returns the Boolean value.

```text

```

Unless the object is specified, it will use the current active object, for example:

```text

```

##### Checking a visited State

You can use following ALH API to check the visited state. You can check whether a specified State was visited by calling the following command.

```text

```

The following code fragment shows how to check whether a specified State of State Machine, e.g.,*State1*, was visited through ALH API.

```text

```

##### Checking if an activity is being simulated

You can use the script to check if an object performs a specific activity. You can use the fUML object syntax with **object.performs("activityName")**. For example:

- self.performs("Driving");

##### Checking if an object is busy

You can use the script to check if an object performing any behavior. You can use the fUML object syntax with **object.isBusy()**. For example:

- self.isBusy();

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>On this page</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="toc" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="bbc299a6-2be1-4931-b10f-148b22ed0984"><ac:parameter ac:name="maxLevel">4</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><h3 class="auto-cursor-target">Getting the current State of a runtime object</h3><p>You can use the following ALH API to get the current State of a specified object. If the object has more than one active State, it returns the first active state. The API returns the instance of the state.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0993a5d4-0f9c-48ce-9d36-e1b2d5af0b19"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[ALH.getState(object)]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><span>The following code fragment shows how to get the current state of a specified object using ALH API:</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e0dc7604-40cc-47ad-906e-7aa4166d0b78"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[ALH.getState(self);
ALH.getState(self).getName()        //To get name of the state instance use .getName()]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3>Checking the State of an object</h3><p>You can use script to check state of an object. You can use the fUML object syntax, with <strong>object.in(&quot;stateName&quot;)</strong><em>.</em> For example:</p><ul><li>self.in(&quot;Operating&quot;);</li></ul><p>Alternatively, you can check the state of an object using ALH API. <span style="color: rgb(23,43,77);">The API returns the Boolean value.</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d2a7e748-0ab1-47b5-aa35-71a847309a97"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[ALH.inState(object, "stateName")
ALH.inState("stateName")                // Returns true or false.]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>Unless the object is specified, it will use the current active object, for example:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="94bef7e0-c006-4015-bd41-5fe5b115b3b4"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[if (ALH.inState("Operating")) {
	force=ccForce
} else {
	force = acc*2
}]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3 class="auto-cursor-target">Checking a visited State</h3><p>You can use following ALH API to check the visited state. You can check whether a specified State was visited by calling the following command.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="78465268-4a73-4879-a5dd-b908c37b2173"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[ALH.wasInState("stateName")]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">The following code fragment shows how to check whether a specified State of State Machine, e.g., </span><em style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">State1</em><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">, was visited through ALH API.</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="4b996387-85c6-4eb8-930c-8f16c5dc2c81"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[ALH.wasInState("State1");	// Returns true or false.]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3>Checking if an activity is being simulated</h3><p>You can use the script to check if an object performs a specific activity. You can use the fUML object syntax with <strong>object.performs(&quot;activityName&quot;)</strong>. For example:</p><ul><li>self.performs(&quot;Driving&quot;);</li></ul><h3>Checking if an object is busy</h3><p>You can use the script to check if an object performing any behavior. You can use the fUML object syntax with <strong>object.isBusy()</strong>. For example:</p><ul><li>self.isBusy();</li></ul></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````
